llm_traces.reason_for_request: 根据整份目录页重建目录树，并识别尾注容器与子项
llm_traces.request_prompt:
你会依次看到一整份书籍目录页截图。请按整份目录的真实组织方式输出 JSON 对象，不要只做扁平标题提取。输出格式必须是 {"endnotes_summary":{"present":false,"container_title":null,"container_printed_page":null,"container_visual_order":null,"has_chapter_keyed_subentries_in_toc":false,"subentry_pattern":null},"items":[{"title":"章节标题","depth":0,"visual_order":1,"printed_page":12,"role_hint":"chapter","parent_title":"Part One"}]}。规则：1. 必须保留无页码的容器层，例如 Part、Book、COURS、INDICES、APPENDICES；这些条目是 container，不是 chapter。2. chapter 是正文里最独立的可导航单元：显式编号章，以及有正文页码的 Introduction、Conclusion、Préambule、单独 Epilogue。chapter 挂在容器下时仍然是 chapter，不会因为容器存在而变成 section。3. front_matter 只用于第一条正文 chapter 之前的辅助材料，如 Acknowledgments、Remerciements、Preface、Foreword、List of Abbreviations、List of Illustrations。4. back_matter 只用于书末参考性材料，如 Bibliography、References、Index、Indices、Works Cited，以及单行独立的 Note on Sources。5. post_body 只用于最后一条正文 chapter 之后、back_matter 之前的实质性内容，如 RÉSUMÉ、SITUATION、Postambule、独立带页码的 Appendix。6. 书末独立的 Notes、Endnotes、Notes on the Text、Notes to the Chapters、Chapter Notes 是 endnotes 容器，不改变正文 chapter 的层级基准；Bibliography、References、Index 永远不要标成 endnotes。7. Note on Sources 只有在下方有按章 notes 子项时才允许判成 endnotes；如果只是单行独立条目，固定判成 back_matter。8. 如果目录里列出了尾注容器下按章分组的子条目，这些子条目标成 section，parent_title 写尾注容器标题。9. 目录页总标题如 Contents、Sommaire 不输出；标题下方的说明性斜体副标题、摘要句、时间说明如果不是独立可导航标题，不输出。10. parent_title 只写直接父标题；最顶层为空字符串；没有可见页码就把 printed_page 设为 null；请保持从前到后、从上到下的视觉顺序。已抽到的目录标题草稿如下，请优先沿用可见标题原文和顺序，但可以补回漏掉的容器层：
- Mad acts, mad speech, and mad people in Chinese medicine and law
- Prologue Diversity, coherence, and scheme transfers
- Psycho-behavioral pathology in Chinese medicine Concepts and early developments, 2nd century BCE – ca. 1150
- From Wind to mucus and Fire The innovations of Jin and Yuan physicians, ca. 1150-1360
- “Danxi said…” The textual life of medical ideas, 1360-1530
- Replenish or attack? Competing etiologies and therapies of madness, 1530-1650
- Shapes of practices Medical trends surrounding dian and kuang, 1650-1911
- Ghosts or mucus? Possession symptoms and their treatment in Ming and Qing medicine
- Gendered mania Women’s madness in late-imperial medicine
- Prologue Language, objects, and practices

auto_applied.note_text: In the Inner Canon and later sources for more than a thousand years, dian 癲/顛 had mostly meant “falling sickness,” an epileptiform disorder characterized by seizures and convulsions. Qing legislative and judicial practices were far from simple, but criminal trials followed common procedures and referred to a single body of laws that discussed madness and mad acts with consistent terminology. Although Qing laws were often modified, officials worked with only one code. Medical texts form a less cohesive corpus. They were produced in often-obscure contexts by authors or compilers who shared neither a common vocabulary nor a common understanding of madness. With no institution to stabilize it, the meaning of medical terms varied in time, between works of the same period, and even within individual works. $ ^{1} $ Unlike the relatively simple madness that the law described, medical kuang 狂 was characterized by a wide variety of behaviors and traced to origins as diverse as mucus accumulation in the chest, internally generated Liver Fire, and invasion by external Wind. Many contradictory explanations could even be presented as true in a single text. $ ^{2} $ To assess and explain this diversity properly, I pay attention not only to trendsetters ..." }, { "page_no": 41, "ocr_excerpt": "task. Yet case records remind us that illness meanings were more than organic. As an affliction that was defined socially, insanity was always more than a bodily event. $ ^{6} $ Categories and scripts channeled phenomena into intelligible and manageable forms; they also guided perception and suggested methods to tackle the reality they purported to describe. Broadly speaking, medical specialists attributed mad symptoms either to invasion by external forces (“deviant Qi”, Wind, ghosts) or to internal imbalances strong enough to provoke illness. $ ^{7} $ Doctors claimed they could cure their patients by expelling these external invaders or redressing these pathogenic imbalances. Because of this emphasis on dynamic situations and a general therapeutic optimism, the medical tradition considered permanent disorders anomalous. Chinese physicians therefore rarely medicalized personality and almost never called patients “madmen” (狂人 or 瘋子). The medical scripts by which doctors understood madness changed significantly between the eighth and the fourteenth century, and again from the fourteenth to the sixteenth century. I plan to demonstrate that new explanations and treatments for insanity ..." }, { "page_no": 93, "ocr_excerpt": "recommendations occupied a central place in Zhu Zhenheng's fourteenth-century medical synthesis, which in turn shaped later medical doctrines. Liu's discussions also contributed to making dian and kuang two quasi identical illnesses. Liu Wansu was born in northern China under Song Huizong (r. 1100-1126), the famous painter-emperor whose sumptuous reign ended when he lost half of his empire to northern invaders, the Jurchens who founded the Jin dynasty in 1115. $ ^{1} $ From his mid-twenties, Liu pored through Wang Bing's annotated version of the Basic Questions and became engrossed in the complexity of its immensely influential cosmological chapters. $ ^{2} $ To those tenacious enough to decipher them, these chapters explained the convoluted workings of the “five circulatory phases and six seasonal influences” (wuyun liuqi 五運六氣; often referred to as yunqi) as they applied to medicine. $ ^{3} $ The “five circulatory phases” were Wood, Fire, Earth, Metal, and Water, the Five Agents familiar to those who have studied any aspect of Chinese thought. The six types of climatic Qi were Wind 風, Heat 熱, Dampness 濕, Fire 火 (or Summer Heat 署), Dryness 燥, and Cold 寒. The problem of change had f ..." }, { "page_no": 94, "ocr_excerpt": "processes and to intervene effectively in these processes. Doctors claimed that yunqi schemes helped them to understand the infinite transformations of illness and to know how to reestablish the dynamic balance that defined good health. Yunqi doctrines became the rage in medical circles soon after the imperial edition of the Basic Questions appeared in the late 11 $ ^{th} $ century, and reached a golden age when they were patronized by Huizong, who considered these cosmic cycles as key to health, government, and cosmic harmony (Yan Liang 2000). $ ^{4} $ Like so many practitioners from the late eleventh century onwards, Liu Wansu treated the yunqi chapters as the most important in the Basic Questions. He even claimed that \"the essential of medical teachings lies in the wnyun liuqi\" (醫教要乎五運六氣), and made this emphasis evident in his Treatise on the Essential Purport of Yunqi in the Inner Canon (Neijing yunqi yaozhi lun 內經運氣要旨論). $ ^{5} $ Liu's preface (Liu WYQ: 195) to this book stressed how subtle and confounding this system of transformations could be to the non-initiated. In several of his works, Liu called wuyun liuqi a “Mysterious ..." }, { "page_no": 95, "ocr_excerpt": "became the mainstay of Liu's most influential book: the Method for Tracing Illnesses to their Origins with the Mysterious Mechanism of the Basic Questions (Suwen xuanji yuanbing shi 素問玄機原 病式; hereafter Yuanbing shi).⁷ There, Liu alleged that “in the method for distinguishing illnesses, it can be seen clearly that the Qi of [all] illnesses can be reduced to transformations of the five phases and six Qi” (識病之法 以其病氣歸於五運六氣之化 明可見矣). Liu Wansu used the fluid interactions between the Five Agents as a guide for scripting both physiological and pathological processes. $ ^{8} $ The Five Agents and the five Yin viscera (五臟) interacted in a way illustrated synoptically in Figure 4.1 (next page). This system was homeostatic (it had an inherent tendency to equilibrium) but dynamic. Since all of its elements were inter-related, a slight imbalance (be it excess or deficiency) in any of the Five Agents or organs led to illnesses that could affect the entire organism. By a sequence of effects predicted by the model, an excess of emotions could, for instance, lead to hemiplegia: Hemiplegia caused by Wind strokes [occurs] because Cardiac Fire abruptly becomes excessive, and [Kidney] Water decays and i ..." }, { "page_no": 96, "ocr_excerpt": "By explaining illness on the plane of the Five Agents, Liu severed the causal chain from the organs themselves.

Using the compelling conceptual architecture of the wuyun liuqi, the Yuanbing shi seemed to make sense of everything: why vomit is acidic, why we have runny noses, why plump persons suffer from more strokes, why some people speak in their sleep, why we laugh when we are tickled, and even why we don't laugh when we tickle ourselves. $ ^{10} $ Liu's ambitious treatise was in fact an extensive commentary on a passage from chapter 74 of the Basic Questions (one of the seven \"Grand treatises\") where Qibo explained the origins of 19 disorders to satisfy the Yellow Emperor's wish to \"hear about the" }, { "page_no": 97, "ocr_excerpt": "mechanisms of illness”聞病機何如. $ ^{11} $ Qibo traced one illness each to the five Yin viscera, one each to the lower and upper body, and twelve to various aspects of Qi: five (including kuang) to Fire, four to Heat, and one each to Wind, Dampness, and Cold. Extrapolating that the five illnesses the canonical excerpt attributed to the viscera actually originated in the movements of Fire, Liu Wansu took this passage as authoritative proof of the pathological power of Heat and Fire, which now represented 14 out of the original 19 entries—five from the viscera, four from Heat, and five from Fire. The short first section on “the five circulatory phases [as they] govern illness” (五運主病) (1a-3b) discussed the Five Agents as they succeeded each other during the year (Wood, Fire, Earth, Metal, and Water). cluster.chapter_title: From Wind to mucus and Fire

unmatched_anchors.source_text: processes and to intervene effectively in these processes. Doctors claimed that yunqi schemes helped them to understand the infinite transformations of illness and to know how to reestablish the dynamic balance that defined good health. Yunqi doctrines became the rage in medical circles soon after the imperial edition of the Basic Questions appeared in the late 11 $ ^{th} $ century, and reached a golden age when they were patronized by Huizong, who considered these cosmic cycles as key to health, government, and cosmic harmony (Yan Liang 2000). $ ^{4} $

Like so many practitioners from the late eleventh century onwards, Liu Wansu treated the yunqi chapters as the most important in the Basic Questions. He even claimed that "the essential of medical teachings lies in the wnyun liuqi" (醫教要乎五運六氣), and made this emphasis evident in his Treatise on the Essential Purport of Yunqi in the Inner Canon (Neijing yunqi yaozhi lun 內經運氣要旨論). $ ^{5} $ Liu's preface (Liu WYQ: 195) to this book stressed how subtle and confounding this system of transformations could be to the non-initiated. Few indeed could awaken to its meaning (悟得其意者鮮矣).

Using the compelling conceptual architecture of the wuyun liuqi, the Yuanbing shi seemed to make sense of everything: why vomit is acidic, why we have runny noses, why plump persons suffer from more strokes, why some people speak in their sleep, why we laugh when we are tickled, and even why we don't laugh when we tickle ourselves. $ ^{10} $

mechanisms of illness”聞病機何如. $ ^{11} $ Qibo traced one illness each to the five Yin viscera, one each to the lower and upper body, and twelve to various aspects of Qi: five (including kuang) to Fire, four to Heat, and one each to Wind, Dampness, and Cold.
The Wind section of the Yuanbing shi contained only one entry, and it made no mention of Wind as a real gale that swirled inside the body. $ ^{13} $ Liu argued instead that Wind

was not air, but an internal form of Qi that appeared mostly because of internally generated Heat and acted in concert with Heat to produce morbid symptoms. As he would state in a later work, “what we speak of as ‘Wind’ is [in fact] ‘Heat’” (凡言風者熱也), because “Wind originally is born from Heat; Heat is the root, Wind the top branch [the superficial manifestation]” (風本生於熱 以熱為本 以風為標). $ ^{14} $

A reader of Liu's treatise would have had no inkling that madness had once been classified as a "Wind illness." Following the passage from the Basic Questions that he was commenting upon, Liu discussed madness under the "Fire" section, where he gave kuang a strong moral meaning: "kuang' means wild unruliness, with no correct or fixed [behavior]; 'yuè' means violating rites and laws and having lost one's normal state" (狂者 狂亂而無正定 越者 乖越禮法而失常也). $ ^{15} $

onset of madness as follows: “Kidney-Water governs the Will, and Water and Fire are opposed to each other. Therefore when Cardiac Fire is overabundant, Renal Water decays: one thus ‘loses one’s Will’ and becomes crazy” (臀水主志而水火相反 故心火旺則腎水衰 乃失志而狂越). $ ^{16} $ Liu also thought that excessive emotions caused by Heat or Fire could trigger madness. $ ^{17} $

Liu Wansu was born in northern China under Song Huizong (r. 1100-1126), the famous painter-emperor whose sumptuous reign ended when he lost half of his empire to northern invaders, the Jurchens who founded the Jin dynasty in 1115. $ ^{1} $ From his mid-twenties, Liu pored through Wang Bing's annotated version of the Basic Questions and became engrossed in the complexity of its immensely influential cosmological chapters. $ ^{2} $ To those tenacious enough to decipher them, these chapters explained the convoluted workings of the “five circulatory phases and six seasonal influences” (wuyun liuqi 五運六氣; often referred to as yunqi) as they applied to medicine. $ ^{3} $ The “five circulatory phases” were Wood, Fire, Earth, Metal, and Water, the Five Agents familiar to those who have studied any aspect of Chinese thought. The six types of climatic Qi were Wind 風, Heat 熱, Dampness 濕, Fire 火 (or Summer Heat 署), Dryness 燥, and Cold 寒.

The diffusion of medical ideas took place in ways more complex than we can ever document. Knowledge and skills were transmitted through local teaching networks, some hereditary, others informal, and yet others based on strict ritual relations between a master and his (rarely her) disciples. $ ^{2} $ The vast majority of teachers remain undocumented. We know almost nothing about the routes by which medical books circulated throughout the empire, carried by traders, officials en route to their posts, or wandering physicians. The role of manuscripts and hand copies of printed books can only be surmised from notes made en passant in various writings, and the private letters that contributed to spreading new medical conceptions have almost all disappeared. $ ^{3} $ Finally we know too little about the oral teachings

Medicine is the most complex field I shall be studying. Qing legislative and judicial practices were far from simple, but criminal trials followed common procedures and referred to a single body of laws that discussed madness and mad acts with consistent terminology. Although Qing laws were often modified, officials worked with only one code. Medical texts form a less cohesive corpus. They were produced in often-obscure contexts by authors or compilers who shared neither a common vocabulary nor a common understanding of madness. With no institution to stabilize it, the meaning of medical terms varied in time, between works of the same period, and even within individual works. $ ^{1} $ Unlike the relatively simple madness that the law described, medical kuang 狂 was characterized by a wide variety of behaviors and traced to origins as diverse as mucus accumulation in the chest, internally generated Liver Fire, and invasion by external Wind. Many contradictory explanations could even be presented as true in a single text. $ ^{2} $

The notion that illnesses were constantly changing started to emerge in the late eleventh century when a few physicians applied to other disorders models that had been designed to understand Cold Injury. A prototypical case of Cold Injury unfolded in distinct stages that each called for a different treatment. Xu Shuwei's 許叔微 (1079-1154) Ninety Discussions on Cold Injury (Shanghan jiushi lun 傷寒九十論), written at the turn in the twelfth century but only printed for the first time in the nineteenth century, were early accounts of successful treatments based on unique diagnoses. $ ^{2} $

The six types of climatic Qi were Wind 風, Heat 熱, Dampness 濕, Fire 火 (or Summer Heat 署), Dryness 燥, and Cold 寒.

By the early seventeenth century, “Danxi”—the label by which doctors referred to precepts and methods they attributed to Zhu Zhenheng 朱震亨 (1282-1358; 4.4)—was no longer the awe-inspiring name it had been a hundred years earlier, and the “Danxi corpus” no longer shaped medical debates. $ ^{2} $ Physicians gave less credence to Danxi’s views of vitality.

page_contexts.ocr_excerpt: ## 2 Prologue to Part One Diversity, coherence, and scheme transfer Medicine is the most complex field I shall be studying. Qing legislative and judicial practices were far from simple, but criminal trials followed common procedures and referred to a single body of laws that discussed madness and mad acts with consistent terminology. Although Qing laws were often modified, officials worked with only one code. Medical texts form a less cohesive corpus. They were produced in often-obscure contexts by authors or compilers who shared neither a common vocabulary nor a common understanding of madness. With no institution to stabilize it, the meaning of medical terms varied in time, between works of the same period, and even within individual works. $ ^{1} $ Unlike the relatively simple madness that the law described, medical kuang 狂 was characterized by a wide variety of behaviors and traced to origins as diverse as mucus accumulation in the chest, internally generated Liver Fire, and invasion by external Wind. Many contradictory explanations could even be presented as true in a single text. $ ^{2} $ To assess and explain this diversity properly, I pay attention not only to trendsetters and famous doctors, but also to writings that epitomized their age more than they marked it: $ ^{3} $ primers, florilegia of medical oddities, unpublished collections of medical cases, etc. In additio ...

task. Yet case records remind us that illness meanings were more than organic. As an affliction that was defined socially, insanity was always more than a bodily event. $ ^{6} $ Categories and scripts channeled phenomena into intelligible and manageable forms; they also guided perception and suggested methods to tackle the reality they purported to describe. Broadly speaking, medical specialists attributed mad symptoms either to invasion by external forces (“deviant Qi”, Wind, ghosts) or to internal imbalances strong enough to provoke illness. $ ^{7} $ Doctors claimed they could cure their patients by expelling these external invaders or redressing these pathogenic imbalances. Because of this emphasis on dynamic situations and a general therapeutic optimism, the medical tradition considered permanent disorders anomalous. Chinese physicians therefore rarely medicalized personality and almost never called patients “madmen” (狂人 or 瘋子). The medical scripts by which doctors understood madness changed significantly between the eighth and the fourteenth century, and again from the fourteenth to the sixteenth century. I plan to demonstrate that new explanations and treatments for insanity often borrowed from the prototypical scenarios of other illnesses or from contemporaneous interpretations of human vitality rather than from the observation of mad patients. recommendations occupied a central place in Zhu Zhenheng's fourteenth-century medical synthesis, which in turn shaped later medical doctrines. Liu's discussions also contributed to making dian and kuang two quasi identical illnesses. Liu Wansu was born in northern China under Song Huizong (r. 1100-1126), the famous painter-emperor whose sumptuous reign ended when he lost half of his empire to northern invaders, the Jurchens who founded the Jin dynasty in 1115. $ ^{1} $ From his mid-twenties, Liu pored through Wang Bing's annotated version of the Basic Questions and became engrossed in the complexity of its immensely influential cosmological chapters. $ ^{2} $ To those tenacious enough to decipher them, these chapters explained the convoluted workings of the “five circulatory phases and six seasonal influences” (wuyun liuqi 五運六氣; often referred to as yunqi) as they applied to medicine. $ ^{3} $ The “five circulatory phases” were Wood, Fire, Earth, Metal, and Water, the Five Agents familiar to those who have studied any aspect of Chinese thought. The six types of climatic Qi were Wind 風, Heat 熱, Dampness 濕, Fire 火 (or Summer Heat 署), Dryness 燥, and Cold 寒. The problem of change had fascinated Chinese thinkers since at least the beginning of the written tradition. Wuyun liuqi provided tools to detect regularities within unfolding

processes and to intervene effectively in these processes. Doctors claimed that yunqi schemes helped them to understand the infinite transformations of illness and to know how to reestablish the dynamic balance that defined good health. Yunqi doctrines became the rage in medical circles soon after the imperial edition of the Basic Questions appeared in the late 11 $ ^{th} $ century, and reached a golden age when they were patronized by Huizong, who considered these cosmic cycles as key to health, government, and cosmic harmony (Yan Liang 2000). $ ^{4} $ Like so many practitioners from the late eleventh century onwards, Liu Wansu treated the yunqi chapters as the most important in the Basic Questions. He even claimed that "the essential of medical teachings lies in the wnyun liuqi" (醫教要乎五運六氣), and made this emphasis evident in his Treatise on the Essential Purport of Yunqi in the Inner Canon (Neijing yunqi yaozhi lun 內經運氣要旨論). $ ^{5} $ Liu's preface (Liu WYQ: 195) to this book stressed how subtle and confounding this system of transformations could be to the non-initiated. Few indeed could awaken to its meaning (悟得其意者鮮矣). In several of his works, Liu called wuyun liuqi a “Mysterious Mechanism” (玄機), from a Taoist cosmological term that did not itself appear in the Inner Canon. $ ^{6} $ This notion

became the mainstay of Liu's most influential book: the Method for Tracing Illnesses to their Origins with the Mysterious Mechanism of the Basic Questions (Suwen xuanji yuanbing shi 素問玄機原 病式; hereafter Yuanbing shi).⁷ There, Liu alleged that “in the method for distinguishing illnesses, it can be seen clearly that the Qi of [all] illnesses can be reduced to transformations of the five phases and six Qi” (識病之法 以其病氣歸於五運六氣之化 明可見矣). Liu Wansu used the fluid interactions between the Five Agents as a guide for scripting both physiological and pathological processes. $ ^{8} $ The Five Agents and the five Yin viscera (五臟) interacted in a way illustrated synoptically in Figure 4.1 (next page). This system was homeostatic (it had an inherent tendency to equilibrium) but dynamic. Since all of its elements were inter-related, a slight imbalance (be it excess or deficiency) in any of the Five Agents or organs led to illnesses that could affect the entire organism. By a sequence of effects predicted by the model, an excess of emotions could, for instance, lead to hemiplegia: Hemiplegia caused by Wind strokes [occurs] because Cardiac Fire abruptly becomes excessive, and [Kidney] Water decays and is unable to curb it. Fire can thus overcome Metal, as a result of which Metal can no longer overcome Wood: Liver Wood thus becomes excessive of its own and joins with the heat of the Fire, causing [th ...

By explaining illness on the plane of the Five Agents, Liu severed the causal chain from the organs themselves.
Fig. 4.1 Mutual conquest and mutual production cycles of the Five Agents and their associated viscera. (This diagram represents the system in a balanced state.)
Using the compelling conceptual architecture of the wuyun liuqi, the Yuanbing shi seemed to make sense of everything: why vomit is acidic, why we have runny noses, why plump persons suffer from more strokes, why some people speak in their sleep, why we laugh when we are tickled, and even why we don't laugh when we tickle ourselves. $ ^{10} $ Liu's ambitious treatise was in fact an extensive commentary on a passage from chapter 74 of the Basic Questions (one of the seven "Grand treatises") where Qibo explained the origins of 19 disorders to satisfy the Yellow Emperor's wish to "hear about the

mechanisms of illness”聞病機何如. $ ^{11} $ Qibo traced one illness each to the five Yin viscera, one each to the lower and upper body, and twelve to various aspects of Qi: five (including kuang) to Fire, four to Heat, and one each to Wind, Dampness, and Cold. Extrapolating that the five illnesses the canonical excerpt attributed to the viscera actually originated in the movements of Fire, Liu Wansu took this passage as authoritative proof of the pathological power of Heat and Fire, which now represented 14 out of the original 19 entries—five from the viscera, four from Heat, and five from Fire. The short first section on “the five circulatory phases [as they] govern illness” (五運主病) (1a-3b) discussed the Five Agents as they succeeded each other during the year (Wood, Fire, Earth, Metal, and Water). The rest of the work was devoted to “the six Qi [as they] bring about illness” (六氣為病). Heat (4b-31a) and Fire (33a-57b) made up almost three quarters of the entire book. $ ^{12} $ Liu took the six Qi not as climatic influences (their traditional meaning), but largely as manifestations of internal imbalances. The close attention he paid to internal pathological processes makes him representative of a larger trend in Chinese medical thinking that I started to explore in the previous chapter when I showed that Song physicians increasingly explained Wind illnesses by internal processes govern ...

was not air, but an internal form of Qi that appeared mostly because of internally generated Heat and acted in concert with Heat to produce morbid symptoms. As he would state in a later work, “what we speak of as ‘Wind’ is [in fact] ‘Heat’” (凡言風者熱也), because “Wind originally is born from Heat; Heat is the root, Wind the top branch [the superficial manifestation]” (風本生於熱 以熱為本 以風為標). $ ^{14} $ Liu Wansu's descriptions of the pathogenesis of a large number of illnesses gave a central place to internal Fire and Heat (instead of Wind). Liu's focus on Heat also explains his predilection for the use of cooling drugs, many of which he drew from the Treatise on Cold Injury. Liu's reinterpretation of Wind as inner Heat contributed to the collapse of the notion of "Wind illnesses" (see sections 4.2, 4.4, and 4.7 below), which in turn helped kuang to become one among many "miscellaneous illnesses" (雜病) that could be discussed independently. A reader of Liu's treatise would have had no inkling that madness had once been classified as a "Wind illness." unmatched_anchors.source_text: To understand how the Danxi corpus was formed, we need to identify the textual provenance of the statements found therein. Different levels of inter-textuality can be distinguished. “Interdiscursivity” is the unwitting yet inevitable “intertext” that literary critiques identify even in the works of the most original authors. $ ^{4} $ Some consider it to be constitutive of any discourse, spoken or written. “Manifest intertextuality” occurs when writers explicitly attribute a passage to another source. The Ming medical tradition was intertextual in both of these senses, but here I pay particular attention to a third and somewhat different kind of intertextuality, not only in the way texts deployed common cultural motifs or overtly referred to previous works, but in the very way texts were produced—intertextuality as a method for assembling texts. $ ^{5} $

Zbezbong is difficult to translate. One of the earliest meanings of zbe 折 was “to break” or “to snap.” In legal language, zbe yu 折獄 meant “judging [i.e., deciding] a criminal case.” $ ^{6} $ But zbe in zbe yu also referred to the weighing and pondering that led to the final decision. $ ^{7} $ Zbe zbong (折中 or 折衷) itself implied the balanced assessment of various positions. $ ^{8} $ We may gloss zbezbong as to resolve—a case, a point of doctrine, etc.—by arbitrating between various positions, often on the basis of another authority. $ ^{9} $

Zbezbong is difficult to translate. One of the earliest meanings of zbe 折 was “to break” or “to snap.” In legal language, zbe yu 折獄 meant “judging [i.e., deciding] a criminal case.” $ ^{6} $ But zbe in zbe yu also referred to the weighing and pondering that led to the final decision. $ ^{7} $ Zbe zbong (折中 or 折衷) itself implied the balanced assessment of various positions. $ ^{8} $ We may gloss zbezbong as to resolve—a case, a point of doctrine, etc.—by arbitrating between various positions, often on the basis of another authority. $ ^{9} $

Intertextual analysis also reveals why medical knowledge was not simply “passed on” from one generation to the other. It shows that the illness scripts that interest us were more than culturally informed responses to observed symptoms. These scripts also emerged and developed intertextually. Finally, the zbezzhong mode of interpreting the past largely explains why Chinese medical thinking often appears so “syncretistic” and “integrative.” $ ^{10} $

The result of zbezzhong writing was not a simple average of previous teachings, it was a coherent amalgamation of several approaches to illness. Zbezzhong was deliberate syncretism—-eclecticism by design, not by default. Focusing on the intertextuality inherent in the zbezzhong approach avoids treating changes in medical doctrine as radical departures from predecessors or as the unfolding of realizations in the consciousness of a series of unrelated authors. Intertextual analysis also reveals why medical knowledge was not simply “passed on” from one generation to the other. It shows that the illness scripts that interest us were more than culturally informed responses to observed symptoms. These scripts also emerged and developed intertextually. Finally, the zbezzhong mode of interpreting the past largely explains why Chinese medical thinking often appears so “syncretistic” and “integrative.” $ ^{10} $

This chapter explores the way medical texts were composed and handed down, the history of important medical doctrines, and the formation and transmission of medical ideas on madness. I first analyze the most elaborate section on madness of all pre-1500 Chinese medical treatises—the section on kuang of a book by Zhu Zhenheng's disciple Lou Ying 櫻英 (1320-1400)—to show how an erudite physician selected from a rich textual heritage to construct a coherent view of illness. I then discuss how another illustrious disciple of Zhu's, Dai Sigong 戴思恭 (1324-1405), contributed to giving prestige to the Danxi approach. Next, I explain how two books that Liu Chun 劉純 (1340-1412), whose father had been Zhu's pupil, compiled in Shaanxi in the late fourteenth century typified the zhezhong approach to the past and further helped to diffused Danxi medicine. In a fourth section, I explain the emergence of the core works of the Danxi corpus in the late fifteenth century and the evolution of their sections on madness. The final two parts are about two writers whose works based on Danxi texts were widely debated and cited in the decades that followed their death. These writers were Wang Lun 王綸 (1453-1510), from Ningbo, and Yu Tuan 虞搏 (1438-1517), from the same locale as Zhu in Zhejiang. Though I focus on individual writers, I insist that they were responding to each other and explain how each of them proposed different interpretations of a common body of texts that included the Danxi corpus, but also the Inner Canon and the works of Liu Wansu and Li Gao. $ ^{11} $ Throughout, I try to recreate the doctrinal debates that make changes in their approaches to madness intelligible.

The only early-Ming treatise that contained a substantial section on madness was Lou Ying's Systematic Outline of Medical Learning (Yixue gangmu). This work exemplifies how a highly literate practitioner could devise approaches to madness from the rich array of texts that were available to him. Lou Ying lived at the "Immortals' crag" (仙岩) in Xiaoshan 蕭山, a town located southeast of Hangzhou across the Qiantang River on an extension of the Grand Canal. Probably introduced by his father Lou Youxian 友賢, Lou Ying studied medicine under Zhu Zhenheng's guidance. $ ^{13} $ In 1362, a little less than four years after he attended Zhu's funeral, Lou opened a school where he received disciples. $ ^{14} $ He became famous enough to be summoned to Nanjing in 1377 and 1381 to treat the emperor (Zhu Yuanzhang, of the newly established Ming dynasty), but he refused permanent employment at court both times. $ ^{15} $

rebind_candidates.anchor_excerpt: I first present a few important trends in Chinese medical thinking and practice from the seventeenth to the nineteenth century. After showing that the mucus etiology remained dominant over that period, I show how resilient and ubiquitous the script of blockage of the Heart by mucus was. I map the vivid “imagination of mucus” $ ^{1} $ through which physicians—from treatise writers to village healers—continued to envision madness from the seventeenth to the nineteenth century. I then turn to a pivotal figure in eighteenth-century medicine—Ye Gui 葉桂 (1667-1746)—and to what an influential work attributed to him said about

average of 4 cases per century and barely more than 2 if we exclude the last hundred years I cover. I found only two relevant cases between 1588 and 1821. The 35 cases represent the way elite doctors (mostly from the Lower Yangzi region) explained the way they understood and treated mad women. They give only a small glimpse of how society handled women who were called mad. Indeed, the main treatment for manic women may not have been medical. There is too little evidence to tell, but home confinement was certainly an option, though it may have come only after the failure of medical or exorcistic treatments. Since the documents tell us precious little about the way female patients experienced their own condition, we are also deprived of the “first-person” data that have informed recent studies of women in Chinese history. $ ^{2} $ I will remind the reader of these limits when I propose tentative conclusions or try to point to possible trends.

Madness and mad people in the Qing dynasty have been studied mostly in a legal context. $ ^{1} $ The easy availability and relative intelligibility of legal documents vis-à-vis medical ones have undoubtedly contributed to this trend. $ ^{2} $ Following pioneers like Chen Hsiu-fen and Angelika Messner, I showed in Part One that Qing medical approaches to madness were also a legitimate topic. In Part Two, I want to show that the study of Qing legislative and judicial activities surrounding madness can be improved, and gains from being compared with the many other ways in which mad acts, words, and people were handled in both technical and non-technical contexts.

The diffusion of medical ideas took place in ways more complex than we can ever document. Knowledge and skills were transmitted through local teaching networks, some hereditary, others informal, and yet others based on strict ritual relations between a master and his (rarely her) disciples. $ ^{2} $ The vast majority of teachers remain undocumented. We know almost nothing about the routes by which medical books circulated throughout the empire, carried by traders, officials en route to their posts, or wandering physicians. The role of manuscripts and hand copies of printed books can only be surmised from notes made en passant in various writings, and the private letters that contributed to spreading new medical conceptions have almost all disappeared. $ ^{3} $ Finally we know too little about the oral teachings

Different levels of inter-textuality can be distinguished. “Interdiscursivity” is the unwitting yet inevitable “intertext” that literary critiques identify even in the works of the most original authors. $ ^{4} $ Some consider it to be constitutive of any discourse, spoken or written. “Manifest intertextuality” occurs when writers explicitly attribute a passage to another source. The Ming medical tradition was intertextual in both of these senses, but here I pay particular attention to a third and somewhat different kind of intertextuality, not only in the way texts deployed common cultural motifs or overtly referred to previous works, but in the very way texts were produced—intertextuality as a method for assembling texts. $ ^{5} $

The emergence of case files as a distinct genre partly stemmed from this flexible conception of illness. Yet even the best English-language histories of Chinese case records usually leap straight from Xu Shuwei to Wang Ji's Stone-Mountain's Medical Case Files (Shishan yian 石山醫案 [1531]; see 6.2) without discussing the dominance of this conception of illness and style of prescription among Jin and Yuan physicians. $ ^{3} $ This omission introduces inaccuracies in accounts of the development of “medical case files” and obscures the process by which they emerged as an independent genre in the sixteenth century.

For my argument to be convincing, I must therefore demonstrate the ascendancy of Danxi precepts. Discursive dominance requires a certain density of social diffusion. Through texts and people (notably Zhu's own disciples), Zhu Zhenheng's medicine first spread like a rhizome along arteries such as the Grand Canal, feeding on social connections and networks of teachers and readers, growing more densely in some places than others, and presenting different faces in the many areas where it flourished. The Danxi corpus became dominant insofar as many important debates between medical specialists concerned the way in which Danxi's legacy should be interpreted. Physicians appropriated the name "Danxi" as a symbol of authority or used it as a foil to make their own doctrinal emphases appear more distinctive. ### 5.2 Madness in Lou Ying's 樓英 (1320-1400) $ ^{12} $ Yixue gangmu 醫學綱目 (1396) The only early-Ming treatise that contained a substantial section on madness was Lou Ying's Systematic Outline of Medical Learning (Yixue gangmu). This work exemplifies how a highly literate practitioner could devise approaches to madness from the rich array of texts that were available to him. Lou Ying lived at the "Immortals' crag" (仙岩) in Xiaoshan 蕭山, a town located southeast of Hangzhou across the Qiantang River on an extension of the Grand Canal. Probably introduced by his father Lou Youxian 友賢, Lou Ying studied medicine under Zhu Zhenheng's guidance. $ ^{13} $ In 1362, a little less than four years after he attended Zhu's funeral, Lou opened a school where he received disciples. $ ^{14} $ He became famous enough to be summoned to Nanjing in 1377 and 1381 to treat the emperor (Zhu Yuanzhang, of the newly established Ming dynasty), but he refused permanent employment at court both times. $ ^{15} $ Lou Ying started to collect materials for his summa of current medical doctrine and healing methods in the 1360s and completed a first version in 1380; he achieved the final version in 1396. Lou did not have all the books he consulted at hand at any given time. He probably kept adding to his text as he consulted more and more works, something that was made more convenient by the proximity of Hangzhou ( ...

Another reason why Lou took so long to complete his opus was the complexity of its subject matter. Lou opened his preface with a telling statement on “medicine as a learning” (醫之為學): “its Way is extensive, its meaning profound, its books infinitely abundant” (其道博 其義深 其書浩瀚). $ ^{16} $ Although Lou claimed that the essentials of medicine “simply hinge on Yin-Yang and the Five Agents” (其要不過陰陽五行而已), he admitted he took more than thirty years to realize that the myriad transformations of illness emerged from imbalances in Yin-Yang and the Five Agents. $ ^{17} $ Textual montages like Lou's Systematic Outline of Medical Learning were not amateurish endeavors. Composite texts professing to cover all aspects of illness and medicine were actually a new genre of medical writing at which Zhu Zhenheng's disciples excelled. $ ^{18} $ Many books claiming to be for beginners were sophisticated digests of complex medical teachings; they were not easy “primers,” but points of entry into the intricate traditions they summarized. Students of medicine found these compilations all the more useful since readers could not easily access all the texts these works cited. Lou Ying's compilation was concerned with medical practice, not abstract doctrine. As a reasoned anthology of citations, the point of the Systematic Outline of Medical Learning was not to reconcile all medical approaches into a perfectly ...

Zhongjing [Zhang Ji] is detailed on external contamination that affects the interior and the surface or Yin and Yang; Danxi distinguishes himself on inner harm of Blood or Qi [that causes] depletion or repletion; Dongyuan [Li Gao] supports and protects central Qi; Hejian [Liu Wansu] removes the old to create the new; Mr. Qian [Yi] distinguishes clearly between the five Yin viscera; Dairen [Zhang Congzheng] is well versed in the three methods [vomiting, purging, and sweating]. Formularies from past ages are abundant: we can simply say that each has its strengths. 仲景詳外感於表裡陰陽 丹溪獨內傷於血氣虛實 東垣扶護中氣 河間推陳 致新 錢氏分明五臟 戴人熟施三法 凡歷代方書甚衆 皆各有所長耳 Since the same symptoms could occur for different reasons, it was plausible that many texts could contain useful methods of treatment. $ ^{19} $ “The benighted fail to appreciate this principle: their reckless trying [of recipes] on approximate illnesses has led many to an unjust and premature death” (昧者不悟是理 妄試疑似之病 每致夭橫者不少矣). Armed with a definition of illness as a multifaceted and ever-changing entity that was typical of Zhu Zhenheng's followers, $ ^{20} $ Lou proposed an elegant solution to the controversial issue of whether “old recipes” could cure “current illnesses.” “Given that an illness with a single name [can require] different methods of treatment, when people use recipes from past ages to treat illness, their success or failure depends on wheth ...

from the Treatise on Cold Injury (or any other source), for instance, were effective when they were used to treat the kinds of illness they cured best. In typical zbezbong fashion, Lou also used this flexible conception of illness and therapy to justify a syncretic approach to the textual past and to clinical practice. His book counted forty fascicles or chapters. The first nine—all part of a large section called “Yin-Yang and the viscera” 陰陽臟腑部—explained general principles on pathomechanisms, diagnosis, and treatments. Followed twenty fascicles (10-29) describing various illnesses and methods to treat them, four fascicles on Cold Injury (30-3), two on the illnesses of women (34-5), four on those of children (36-9), and one with commentaries on the five periods and six Qi in the Inner Canon (40). $ ^{21} $ In the twenty chapters on disorders other than Cold Injury, Lou Ying classified illnesses into five sections (bu 部) named after the pairs of Yin and Yang viscera with which he associated them. There were six fascicles on the Liver and Gallbladder (肝膽), five on the Heart and Small Intestine (心小腸), five on the Spleen and Stomach (脾胃), two on the Lungs and Large Intestine (肺大腸), and two on the Kidneys and Bladder (腎膀胱).       "ocr_excerpt": "Most elite physicians from the twelfth century onward, while recognizing that there was “constancy” (chang 常) to illnesses, emphasized that the best healers should also react to “transformations” (bian 變), the events that diverged from the regular ways in which reality was supposed to unfold. “Constancy” itself referred not to unchanging essences but to the regular and predictable way in which processes developed. The emergence of case files as a distinct genre partly stemmed from this flexible conception of illness. Yet even the best English-language histories of Chinese case records usually leap straight from Xu Shuwei to Wang Ji's Stone-Mountain's Medical Case Files (Shishan yian 石山醫案 [1531]; see 6.2) without discussing the dominance of this conception of illness and style of prescription among Jin and Yuan physicians. $ ^{3} $ This omission introduces inaccuracies in accounts of the development of “medical case files” and obscures the process by which they emerged as an independent genre in the sixteenth century. unmatched_anchors.source_text: with warming drugs, others kept blaming them for it, as when Xu Yuhe 許豫和 (late 18 $ ^{th} $ century) accused a physician of having exacerbated a madman’s symptoms with calorific replenishing, or when Wu Tang 吴塘 (1758-1836) claimed that another doctor’s attacking of mucus and Fire in a case of insanity had “helped the bandits do harm” (助賊為虐). $ ^{11} $

Although mucus and Fire had long been paired, Liang Xuemeng 梁學孟 in 1607 was perhaps the first to dedicate a section to “mucus Fire” (痰火) in a medical work (his Guoyi zongzhi 國醫宗旨). $ ^{12} $ His Tanbuo zhuanmen 痰火顓門 (1610) became the first work devoted entirely to this topic, $ ^{13} $ followed by Gong Juzhong’s 龔居中 Tanbuo dianxue 痰火點雪 (1630). Zhang Lu also discussed mucus-Fire in his Comprehensive Medicine (Yitong 醫通, 1695), explaining that “formful [i.e., visible and palpable] mucus” (有形之痰) was associated with “formless [invisible] Fire” (無形之火). $ ^{14} $ Cao Cunxin 曹存心 (1767-1834, from Changshu 常熟 in Jiangsu), later posed mucus and Fire as two equivalents: “Mucus is none other than Fire with form, whereas Fire is formless mucus” (痰即有形之火 火即無形之痰). $ ^{15} $

Although mucus and Fire had long been paired, Liang Xuemeng 梁學孟 in 1607 was perhaps the first to dedicate a section to “mucus Fire” (痰火) in a medical work (his Guoyi zongzhi 國醫宗旨). $ ^{12} $ His Tanbuo zhuanmen 痰火顓門 (1610) became the first work devoted entirely to this topic, $ ^{13} $ followed by Gong Juzhong’s 龔居中 Tanbuo dianxue 痰火點雪 (1630). Zhang Lu also discussed mucus-Fire in his Comprehensive Medicine (Yitong 醫通, 1695), explaining that “formful [i.e., visible and palpable] mucus” (有形之痰) was associated with “formless [invisible] Fire” (無形之火). $ ^{14} $ Cao Cunxin 曹存心 (1767-1834, from Changshu 常熟 in Jiangsu), later posed mucus and Fire as two equivalents: “Mucus is none other than Fire with form, whereas Fire is formless mucus” (痰即有形之火 火即無形之痰). $ ^{15} $

During the seventeenth and eighteenth centuries, physicians increasingly asserted the general importance of mucus pathology. Zhang Lu claimed that mucus appeared in seven or eight ill people out of ten (痰飲為患 十人有七八), $ ^{16} $ whereas Cheng Guopeng 程國彭 (from She 歙 county in Huizhou; fl. early 18 $ ^{th} $ century) asserted that “There are no illnesses in which there is no fever or mucus” (凡病未有不發熱 不生痰者). $ ^{17} $

This perceived ubiquity of mucus generated more explanations of its pathological origins. Physicians argued that mucus could stem from an almost infinite diversity of imbalances and failures to circulate. $ ^{18} $ Huang Yuanyu 黄元御 (1705-1758) grounded mucus in Dampness (associated with the Spleen), another common explanation. $ ^{19} $ Chen Xiuyuan 陳修園 (1753-1823), a popularizer of medical doctrines whose writings circulated widely in the second half of the nineteenth century, claimed that mucus was rooted in Water (the phase associated with either the two Kidneys or the left Kidney) and that sticky mucus appeared when Water Qi was “decocted” by excessive Yang (得陽煎熬). $ ^{20} $

rebind_candidates.anchor_excerpt: The concept of sui 崇 is almost as old as the Chinese writing system itself. Donald Harper has glossed sui (in ancient usage) as a calamity brought about by spirits in response to human conduct of which they disapproved. $ ^{5} $ Because of the several layers of meaning it acquired through time, sui is difficult to translate, but for late imperial times I shall gloss it as “specter” or “spectral affliction,” or as “affliction” when it occurs in compounds like xiesui 邪祟 (“affliction by evil”) and guisui 鬼祟 (“demonic affliction”).

Whereas ritual texts that proposed methods to remove these beings gave little detail about how they affected the body, medical sources often described ghosts as entering the body of their victims and occupying specific organs. As such, late-imperial sui (“spectral afflictions”) thus differed from the sui (“calamities”) of the Shang era, which did not depend on control over the body of the victim. $ ^{6} $

First, despite many claims to the contrary, Wang Ji's work was not the first autonomous work to contain the cases of a single physician. $ ^{4} $ Zhang Congzheng's Ten Configurations and Three Therapies (Shixing sanliao 十形三療), an anthology of Zhang's cases, was edited soon after his death (1228) by his disciples, and was circulating on its own by the fourteenth century. $ ^{5} $ Case records were also an important part of Zhu Zhenheng's medical practice even if he did not collate them into a book during his lifetime. $ ^{6} $ Both his biographer

I said: “This is a manifestation of over-repletion, not depletion.” $ ^{171} $ Therefore I used extremely bitter medicine $ ^{172} $ to drain Fire from the two tracts of the Heart and the Gallbladder. $ ^{173} $ When draining the Heart [tract], it is necessary to drain the Small Intestine [tract] as well, $ ^{174} $ because “when the disorder is in a Yin visceral system, regulate its [corresponding] Yang visceral system.” $ ^{175} $ [Moreover,] because the Gallbladder has no outlet, $ ^{176} $ I borrow the Small Intestine as an outlet: [this is] also [a reason] for draining the Small Intestine. $ ^{177} $

I said: “This is a manifestation of over-repletion, not depletion.” $ ^{171} $ Therefore I used extremely bitter medicine $ ^{172} $ to drain Fire from the two tracts of the Heart and the Gallbladder. $ ^{173} $ When draining the Heart [tract], it is necessary to drain the Small Intestine [tract] as well, $ ^{174} $ because “when the disorder is in a Yin visceral system, regulate its [corresponding] Yang visceral system.” $ ^{175} $ [Moreover,] because the Gallbladder has no outlet, $ ^{176} $ I borrow the Small Intestine as an outlet: [this is] also [a reason] for draining the Small Intestine. $ ^{177} $

page_contexts.ocr_excerpt: Most elite physicians from the twelfth century onward, while recognizing that there was “constancy” (chang 常) to illnesses, emphasized that the best healers should also react to “transformations” (bian 變), the events that diverged from the regular ways in which reality was supposed to unfold. “Constancy” itself referred not to unchanging essences but to the regular and predictable way in which processes developed. The emergence of case files as a distinct genre partly stemmed from this flexible conception of illness. Yet even the best English-language histories of Chinese case records usually leap straight from Xu Shuwei to Wang Ji's Stone-Mountain's Medical Case Files (Shishan yian 石山醫案 [1531]; see 6.2) without discussing the dominance of this conception of illness and style of prescription among Jin and Yuan physicians. $ ^{3} $ This omission introduces inaccuracies in accounts of the development of “medical case files” and obscures the process by which they emerged as an independent genre in the sixteenth century. First, despite many claims to the contrary, Wang Ji's work was not the first autonomous work to contain the cases of a single physician. $ ^{4} $ Zhang Congzheng's Ten Configurations and Three Therapies (Shixing sanliao 十形三療), an anthology of Zhang's cases, was edited soon after his death (1228) by his disciples, and was circulating on its own by the fourteenth cen ...

with warming drugs, others kept blaming them for it, as when Xu Yuhe 許豫和 (late 18 $ ^{th} $ century) accused a physician of having exacerbated a madman’s symptoms with calorific replenishing, or when Wu Tang 吴塘 (1758-1836) claimed that another doctor’s attacking of mucus and Fire in a case of insanity had “helped the bandits do harm” (助賊為虐). $ ^{11} $ Medical writing also remained profoundly intertextual. Several treatises from the seventeenth and eighteenth centuries included long and increasingly complex sections on madness. They were longer and denser partly because they could borrow from a larger number of texts, but also because they applied to more disorders than ever the old claim that any illness could be caused by multiple factors. Two representative examples are Feng Zhaozhang's 馮兆張 (from Haiyan 海鹽 in Zhejiang) Jinnang milu zazheng daxiao becan 錦囊祕錄雜證大小合參 (in 20 juan; 1694) and Shen Jin'ao's 沈金鰲 (1717-1776; from Wuxi 無錫 in Jiangsu) Zabing yuanliu xizhu 雜病源流犀燭 (in 30 juan; 1773), both of which concentrated on explaining miscellaneous illnesses. Besides these exhaustive accounts of manifold etiologies, we find attempts to simplify—even unify—medical doctrines, books with titles like Unity in the Forest of Medicine (Luo Denggao's 駱登高 Yilin yizhi 醫林一致; 1703), Knowing Medicine in One Glance (Zhu Shijin's 朱時進 Yijian nengyi 一見能醫; 1769), and Medicine for Sots (Long Huitang's ...

### 7.2 The continued dominance of the mucus etiology In earlier chapters, I examined the rise of mucus in Chinese medical thinking and its eventual use in explanations of madness. I also showed how the Danxi corpus had given pride of place to the mucus etiology in general. On the basis of these now mature theories, seventeenth-century physicians continued to discuss how mucus emerged, what disorders it caused, and how these illnesses should be treated. Some did so in special sections on this viscous intruder, others in commentaries on the notion of “mucus and rheum” (痰飲; 3.5) in Zhang Ji’s Essentials of the Golden Casket (3 $ ^{rd} $ century CE). Although mucus and Fire had long been paired, Liang Xuemeng 梁學孟 in 1607 was perhaps the first to dedicate a section to “mucus Fire” (痰火) in a medical work (his Guoyi zongzhi 國醫宗旨). $ ^{12} $ His Tanbuo zhuanmen 痰火顓門 (1610) became the first work devoted entirely to this topic, $ ^{13} $ followed by Gong Juzhong’s 龔居中 Tanbuo dianxue 痰火點雪 (1630). Zhang Lu claimed that mucus appeared in seven or eight ill people out of ten (痰飲為患 十人有七八), $ ^{16} $ whereas Cheng Guopeng 程國彭 (from She 歙 county in Huizhou; fl. early 18 $ ^{th} $ century) asserted that “There are no illnesses in which there is no fever or mucus” (凡病未有不發熱 不生痰者). $ ^{17} $ This perceived ubiquity of mucus generated more explanations of its pathological origins. Physicians argued that mucus could stem from an almost infinite diversity of imbalances and failures to circulate. $ ^{18} $ Huang Yuanyu 黄元御 (1705-1758) grounded mucus in Dampness (associated with the Spleen), another common explanation. $ ^{19} $ Chen Xiuyuan 陳修園 (1753-1823), a popularizer of medical doctrines whose writings circulated widely in the second half of the nineteenth century, claimed that mucus was rooted in Water (the phase associated with either the two Kidneys or the left Kidney) and that sticky mucus appeared when Water Qi was “decocted” by excessive Yang (得陽煎熬). $ ^{20} $

### 7.3 Shapes of practice: the scope of mucus therapies of madness The above section showed that mucus played an important role in Qing medical discussions of many illnesses. Starting with an examination of seventeenth-century medical primers that were popular under the Qing, let me now demonstrate from many different angles that mucus conceptions also dominated the way doctors thought about insanity. Since the practice of medicine was not regulated, virtually anyone could take it up. All that was needed was a medical primer and a few patients willing to pay for a cure. Physicians who sought a more elite clientele usually needed to claim a teacher and to be able to couch their arguments in classical terms drawn from the medical canons or the Yijing, but the success of medical primers suggests that medicine was easily learned outside of teacher-student relations. The large number of surviving editions of books like Huangfu Zhong's 皇甫中 Enlightened Physician Pointing to His Palm (Mingyi zhizhang 明醫指掌) or Gong Tingxian's Shoushi baoyuan 壽世保元, and the fact that other books lamented their excessive influence, suggest that they guided the practice of more physicians than the treatises modern historians rely on in their studies of Chinese medical thinking. $ ^{21} $ ## Shapers of practice? What seventeenth-century medical manuals said about madness There is no lack of people within an ...

# is limitless and the impoverished roam the markets; $ ^{22} $ [moreover] the meanings and principles [expressed in these books] are profound and subtle, so that the ignorant have failed to examine them. 薄海内外 雅不乏人 而千里之間 博綜淹貫者 僅得一二 何能濟八方之老幼 即曰醫籍在也 卷帙浩淼 貧者弗遑市 意理淵微 愚者未及察 Xu Shirou 許士柔, 1622 preface to Shao Da's 邵達 re-edition of Huangfu Zhong's 皇甫中 Mingyi zhizhang 明醫指掌 $ ^{23} $ As its title indicates, the Enlightened Physician Pointing to His Palm (Mingyi zhi-bang 明醫指掌, 1579), which was re-edited and augmented in 1622, purported to be a digest of authentic medical doctrines and techniques. $ ^{24} $ Compiled in a format that put the way of medicine “in front of one’s eyes” (所謂道在目前; Xu preface), this manual sought to remedy the two problems Xu lamented in his preface: the dispersion of knowledge over too many books, and the difficulty of mastering these convoluted doctrines. In the Enlightened Physician, short and memorable “songs” (ge 歌) on individual illnesses were followed by discussions (lun 論), information on the pulses (mai 脈) most characteristic of these illnesses, and the recipes for recommended formulas. We owe the book's design to a man named Shao Da 邵達, a Suzhou physician whose father had also practiced medicine. $ ^{25} $ Shao recounted how his father had enjoined him

to study Huangfu Zhong's original Enlightened Physician (1579) in order to understand Zhang Ji's Cold-Injury medicine, Li Gao's notion of inner harm, Liu Wansu's Heat disorders, and Zhu Zhenheng's miscellaneous illnesses. $ ^{26} $ As Shao recalled, he learned as much as he could from Huangfu's work, but deplored that it relied on illustrations (圖) at the expense of discussions (論), described the symptoms clearly but said little about pulses, and gave detailed recipes but neglected to explain the therapeutic methods that justified this choice of remedies. $ ^{27} $ Shao also found inconvenient that the recipes appeared in a volume separate from the descriptions and illustrations. $ ^{28} $ In his 1622 edition, Shao eliminated the pictures, regrouped the descriptions of illnesses with the recommended recipes, and added distinctions between the many possible origins of every illness he discussed. Shao Da's version of the Enlightened Physician (in 10 juan) was republished many times throughout the Qing, testifying to its popularity among practitioners, as well as among amateurs who sought a digest of medical knowledge for use at home. A catalog of medical books extant in Chinese libraries indicates that at least 19 Qing editions of Shao Da's work have survived: 2 from the seventeenth century after 1644, 4 from the eighteenth, 10 from the nineteenth, 2 from the early twentieth, and ...

Blithely calling oneself venerable: this is called kuang. 妄自稱尊號曰狂 Dian is a stiff body and an unhappy mind. 癲者身僵心不樂 You should know these two illnesses are Yin and Yang. 须知二證有陰陽 These verses squeezed together the symptoms of dian and kuang as explained in the Basic Questions and the Canon of Problems (problem 59). More focused on current usage, the discussion that followed cited the same passages at more length before quoting an excerpt from the "diankuang" section of the Danxi xinfa and the Canon of Problems' claim that dian was the doubling of Yin and kuang the doubling of Yang. $ ^{30} $ Two sections on the respective symptoms of dian and kuang cited more of the Danxi xinfa, but added two recipes to those that this book had recommended. $ ^{31} $ To this unsystematic rehashing of Danxi medicine, Shao Da added “notes” (an 按) where he attributed madness to the pathological action of Fire and mucus.       "ocr_excerpt": "## 2 Prologue to Part One Diversity, coherence, and scheme transfer Medicine is the most complex field I shall be studying. Qing legislative and judicial practices were far from simple, but criminal trials followed common procedures and referred to a single body of laws that discussed madness and mad acts with consistent terminology. Although Qing laws were often modified, officials worked with only one code. Medical texts form a less cohesive corpus. They were produced in often-obscure contexts by authors or compilers who shared neither a common vocabulary nor a common understanding of madness. With no institution to stabilize it, the meaning of medical terms varied in time, between works of the same period, and even within individual works. $ ^{1} $ Unlike the relatively simple madness that the law described, medical kuang 狂 was characterized by a wide variety of behaviors and traced to origins as diverse as mucus accumulation in the chest, internally generated Liver Fire, and invasion by external Wind.       "ocr_excerpt": "### 6.2 Replenishing madness: Wang Ji's case-based reasoning Wang Ji 汪機 (1463-1540), from Qimen 祁門 county in Huizhou, was one of a handful of sixteenth-century physicians who attributed a case of mad behavior to depletion and treated it accordingly. $ ^{23} $ This section outlines the emergence, in the works of Wang and a few more doctors, of a conception of madness caused by depletion that would become more prominent later in the sixteenth century. I first explain the doctrinal background of Wang's predilection for treating patients with ginseng and astragalus root (黄芪), then I present two of his madness cases in light of his broader orientations. Finally I describe the replenishing therapies that other contemporary physicians deployed to treat madness symptoms. Both in his approach to madness and in his general doctrinal discussions, Wang often tackled issues that were discussed in the contemporary medical literature. The larger community of experts of which he was part tackled issues defined by the Danxi corpus (see Chapter 5) and discussed the writings of illustrious authors like Wang Lun 王綸 (1453-1510; see 5.6). Strongly opposing this admonition, Wang Ji tried to show that calorific replenishers could treat not only cases of Qi depletion (as everyone agreed), but also illnesses that were caused by Blood depletion. Both Wang and Sun had recorded their cures long before they had planned to publish them, and published them too late in their lives to compete for clients. $ ^{15} $ And since the editors of the Categorized Case Files did not include cases of their own in their compilation, they could not have published this book to attract customers. Since only two men – both from Huizhou and in the same intellectual lineage – published a collection of their own cases in the sixteenth century, no wave of yian was unleashed that would require a sociological explanation. $ ^{16} $

which had enjoyed a strong reputation for medical studies since at least the Song, was where the prominent third edition of the Danxi xinfa 丹溪心法 had been published in 1482 and a sequel called Danxi xinfa fuyu 丹溪心法附語 appeared in 1536. In 1556, Huizhou man Xu Chunfu 徐春甫 completed a compendium called Gujin yitong daquan 古今醫統大全, a mosaic of short texts or excerpts, and had it printed in Suzhou the following year. $ ^{17} $ Two editions of Wu Kun's 吴崑 Yifang kao 醫方考 (with a 1584 preface by Wu) were also printed in 1586, whereas Sun Yikui's works came out in the 1580s and 1590s. These Huizhou physicians were supported by a strong intellectual tradition, a network of active printers, and a growing commercial wealth that facilitated the sponsoring of medical books. $ ^{18} $

rebind_candidates.anchor_excerpt: A notorious adage circulating in late imperial medical circles declared that the women's illnesses were more difficult to cure than men's. $ ^{1} $ Two other sayings claimed that women's bodies were "governed by Blood" (以血為主 or a variant) and that treating women necessitated "separate prescriptions" (别方). Ming and Qing physicians cited these sayings to set up concurring arguments, to introduce objections or qualifications, or to clinch points with the help of a well-formulated piece of common knowledge.

Madness and mad people in the Qing dynasty have been studied mostly in a legal context. $ ^{1} $ The easy availability and relative intelligibility of legal documents vis-à-vis medical ones have undoubtedly contributed to this trend. $ ^{2} $ Following pioneers like Chen Hsiu-fen and Angelika Messner, I showed in Part One that Qing medical approaches to madness were also a legitimate topic. In Part Two, I want to show that the study of Qing legislative and judicial activities surrounding madness can be improved, and gains from being compared with the many other ways in which mad acts, words, and people were handled in both technical and non-technical contexts.

Medicine is the most complex field I shall be studying. Qing legislative and judicial practices were far from simple, but criminal trials followed common procedures and referred to a single body of laws that discussed madness and mad acts with consistent terminology. Although Qing laws were often modified, officials worked with only one code. Medical texts form a less cohesive corpus. They were produced in often-obscure contexts by authors or compilers who shared neither a common vocabulary nor a common understanding of madness. With no institution to stabilize it, the meaning of medical terms varied in time, between works of the same period, and even within individual works. $ ^{1} $ Unlike the relatively simple madness that the law described, medical kuang 狂 was characterized by a wide variety of behaviors and traced to origins as diverse as mucus accumulation in the chest, internally generated Liver Fire, and invasion by external Wind. Many contradictory explanations could even be presented as true in a single text. $ ^{2} $

rebind_candidates.anchor_excerpt: Wang Ji was not the only physician of his time who devised new replenishing cures for madness manifested or occasioned by emotional excesses. Wu Qiu 吳球 (fl. 1520-30s), from Taizhou in Zhejiang, cured a woman who had lost her mind and suffered from dian (失志顛疾), an illness Wu defined as a “disorderly heart (or mind)” (亂心).⁴⁷ She would either sob or laugh, wander around naked, or shut the door and speak a lot (或哭或笑 或裸體而走 或閉戶而多言). She was “emaciated and of an anxious nature, and excessive worry had consumed and harmed her Cardiac blood” (瘦弱性急 因思過度 耗傷心血), making the Spirit unable to hold to its lodging.⁴⁸ Wu replenished her with human placenta, which he called “Purple river-chariot” (紫河車) because the best placentas were purportedly purple.⁴⁹ The patient ate the boiled placenta and became well after Wu gave her “Will-fixing pill” (定志丸) and “Heart-replenishing decoction” (補心湯) for 100 days.⁵⁰

For the illnesses [Zhu] treated, what the illness looked like, what excellent remedy he applied, and the drinking of what drugs led to healing [were recorded]. From the past until now, for the efficacious [cases]: who [his patient] was, from what village in what county [he or she came], the names of the interested party, and what [Zhu] had witnessed and heard are clearly documented. $ ^{7} $

Some books speak of “diankuang,” $ ^{109} $ some of “dianxian,” $ ^{110} $ some of “Wind xian,” $ ^{111} $ some of “Wind dian,” some of “fright xian,” whereas some place dian and xian in two [separate] sections. There is no set [way of] discussing [these disorders]. This is because those who speak of dian on its own follow the Inner Canon [Basic Questions], whereas those who speak of dian-xian and dian-kuang follow the Divine Pivot. $ ^{112} $

Many contradictory explanations could even be presented as true in a single text. $ ^{2} $ To assess and explain this diversity properly, I pay attention not only to trendsetters and famous doctors, but also to writings that epitomized their age more than they marked it: $ ^{3} $ primers, florilegia of medical oddities, unpublished collections of medical cases, etc. In additio ...

Dai Liang 戴良 and the author of his epitaph Song Lian 宋濂 concurred that Zhu kept detailed records of his clinical encounters. As Dai explained in a text written in 1358 or 1359: For the illnesses [Zhu] treated, what the illness looked like, what excellent remedy he applied, and the drinking of what drugs led to healing [were recorded]. From the past until now, for the efficacious [cases]: who [his patient] was, from what village in what county [he or she came], the names of the interested party, and what [Zhu] had witnessed and heard are clearly documented. $ ^{7} $ 其所治病凡機病之狀何如 施何良方 飲何藥而愈 自前至今 驗者何人 何縣里 主名 得諸見聞 班班可紀 Song Lian even claimed that Zhu's "disciples possess books [of these records], classified by illnesses" (門人類證有書). $ ^{8} $ It is no coincidence that Zhu Zhenheng's disciples and admirers were the leading transmitters of this flexible art of prescription during the early Ming dynasty. Zhu's own case files played a role. Wang Xing's 王行 preface (dated 1377, less than 20 years after Zhu's death) to a compendium of Zhu's cases explained how unusual Zhu's approach had been. $ ^{9} $ Most physicians, Wang claimed, instructed "to treat such-and-such an illness with this remedy, and that such-and-such a remedy treats such-and-such an illness" (治某病以某方以某方治某病而已). But "illnesses change greatly and are without constancy, whereas recipes are fixed and limited" (病多變而無常 方一定而有限). And h ...

“respond to inconstant illnesses with limited recipes”(以有限之方應無常之病)? $ ^{10} $ Wang Xing attributed these singular doctrines (殊不類常聞也) to the aptly styled Wang Lifang 王 立方 (Wang “Establish-a-prescription”), who owed his distinctive ideas to having been taught by Zhu’s pupil Dai Sigong 戴思恭 (1324-1405; see 5.3). Wang Lifang had allegedly received from Dai himself the cases by Zhu that Wang Xing was prefacing. $ ^{11} $ The success of other practitioners in the “Zhu Danxi” line was also explained by their distinctive refusal to stick to set recipes. Wang Xing attributed the “extraordinary efficacy” (奇驗) of Dai Sigong’s cures to his adoption of Danxi’s flexible methods. According to one of Jiang Wusheng’s 蔣武生 (1351-1424) biographers, Jiang “did not hold to the old recipes, but investigated the root of illnesses and made his own recipes; therefore his treatments were always successful” (不執古方而究病所本 自為方 故所治恆十全). $ ^{12} $ The spread of case records would be difficult to understand without the notion that every instance of illness deserved a unique treatment, and without the prestige associated with this flexible style of prescription. shall see in the next section, Wang Ji, just like both his predecessors (Zhu Zhenheng) and his contemporaries (Xue Ji), used his cases to support broader medical arguments. I would say the “case files” genre emerged because an increasing number of doctors kept records of their clinical encounters, both for their patients and for their own reference. Their record-keeping depended on the notion that each bout of illness potentially required a unique therapy. It would thus seem that the sixteenth-century “bureaucratization” of case files that Cullen documented—and the common likening of medical intervention to judicial reasoning and judgment—was simply the repackaging along new metaphorical lines of a kind of record that had become increasingly important since approximately the thirteenth century, when some elite physicians adopted the flexible conception of illness I just mentioned. $ ^{21} $ Charlotte Furth (2007a: 19) has interpreted the case form (in medicine as well as other fields) as a “strategy of modernists in an intellectual tradition that gave awesome weight to the authority of antiquity,” and medical cases as tools to “negotiate the gap between… medical canon and therapeutic strategy.” $ ^{22} $ Without downplaying the freedom allowed by case files to go beyond canonical formulations, I would contend that, at least in the sixteenth century when they came into widesprea ...

### 6.2 Replenishing madness: Wang Ji's case-based reasoning Wang Ji 汪機 (1463-1540), from Qimen 祁門 county in Huizhou, was one of a handful of sixteenth-century physicians who attributed a case of mad behavior to depletion and treated it accordingly. $ ^{23} $ This section outlines the emergence, in the works of Wang and a few more doctors, of a conception of madness caused by depletion that would become more prominent later in the sixteenth century. I first explain the doctrinal background of Wang's predilection for treating patients with ginseng and astragalus root (黄芪), then I present two of his madness cases in light of his broader orientations. Finally I describe the replenishing therapies that other contemporary physicians deployed to treat madness symptoms. Both in his approach to madness and in his general doctrinal discussions, Wang often tackled issues that were discussed in the contemporary medical literature. The larger community of experts of which he was part tackled issues defined by the Danxi corpus (see Chapter 5) and discussed the writings of illustrious authors like Wang Lun 王綸 (1453-1510; see 5.6). In his work on poxes (Douzhi libian 痘治理辨; preface from 1531), Wang Ji had addressed Wei Zhi's 衛直 recently published Boai xinjian 博愛心鑒 (1525), whereas Wang's work on "external medicine" (Waike lili 外科理例; 1531 preface) integrated arguments he had found in Waike fabui ...

earliest case collections in Chinese medical history, $ ^{25} $ but it also served Wang Ji to make an array of doctrinal points on pathology and human vitality, and to defend his singular clinical approaches. The most distinctive and controversial aspect of Wang's medical practice was his use of calorific drugs (chiefly ginseng and astragalus) to treat cases of depletion that other physicians (following Wang Lun) would have treated with coolants. $ ^{26} $ Wang Lun had attributed visible emissions of Blood to "Fire" resulting from Blood/Yin deficiency, and argued that warming drugs only exacerbated this destructive Fire, and should therefore be proscribed when treating such cases (5.6). Strongly opposing this admonition, Wang Ji tried to show that calorific replenishers could treat not only cases of Qi depletion (as everyone agreed), but also illnesses that were caused by Blood depletion. Wang Ji had to dispute Wang Lun’s authority in his daily medical practice. Not only did rival physicians routinely use coolants to replenish Yin and Blood, it appears that even patients cited Wang Lun’s Miscellaneous Writings of Enlightened Physicians (1502) to challenge Wang Ji’s prescriptions. $ ^{27} $ When he disagreed with a rival’s diagnosis in front of patients and their relatives, Wang Ji sometimes argued that loss of blood, a symptom commonly attributed to Yin/Blood depletion, was in ...

unmatched_anchors.source_text: A network of key categories defined the semantic field of criminal intention in Qing homicide laws. Xin 心, a word I have translated as “Heart” in medical texts, is best rendered as “mind” or “intent” in legal contexts because legal discussion of xin surrounded intentions, not the functioning of any organ. The term xin appeared in expressions like “intentionally” (or “having the intention to”: 有心), “intent upon...” (… 之心) or a combination of both. $ ^{6} $ Yi 意 could be used similarly (as in the phrase “there was initially no intention to harm another” 初無害人之意), but it was also used to describe “unexpected” (出於不意) actions. Yi appeared in the technical phrase “the one who devised the idea [of killing]” (造意者) or in combination with yu 欲 in “there was an intention to kill” (有意欲殺). Gu 故 was used adverbially in “killing intentionally” (故殺).

Unlike modern Western legal systems, which distinguish between murder and manslaughter or between degrees of homicide, Chinese law in imperial times called all homicides “killings” (sha 殺). Legal commentators customarily divided killings into sub-types. In descending order of gravity, the “Six killings” (六殺) were mou sha 謀殺 (“Killing by design” or “Plotting to kill”), gu sha 故殺 (“Killing intentionally”), dou sha 鬭殺 (“Killing in an affray”), wu sha 誤殺 (“Killing by mistake”), xi sha 戲殺 (“Killing in play”), and guoshi sha 過失殺 (“Killing accidentally”). Three statutes (lǔ 律) sufficed to define killings in the Ming and Qing codes. $ ^{7} $ Here I explain how the “Six killings” applied to the killing of a single person unrelated to the killer.

“Killing by design” (謀殺), which was understood as the result of prior scheming (謀) between two or more people, was considered the gravest of the six. $ ^{8} $ The law expected such plots to occur in vendettas, fights over property, or schemes to kill one's own spouse. “The one who devised the idea” (造意者) was to be sentenced to decapitation after the Autumn Assizes (斬監後), where it would be classified as “effective” (情實) even if the main plotter had not taken part in the actual killing. $ ^{9} $ The participation of more than one

“Killing by design” (謀殺), which was understood as the result of prior scheming (謀) between two or more people, was considered the gravest of the six. $ ^{8} $ The law expected such plots to occur in vendettas, fights over property, or schemes to kill one's own spouse. “The one who devised the idea” (造意者) was to be sentenced to decapitation after the Autumn Assizes (斬監後), where it would be classified as “effective” (情實) even if the main plotter had not taken part in the actual killing. $ ^{9} $ The participation of more than one

In the three remaining killings, which were all discussed in a single statute, death was understood as being inflicted involuntarily. $ ^{13} $ “Killing in play” (戲殺) occurred in the course of dangerous activities in which people engaged purposely, like sword-practice. $ ^{14} $ This rare crime was punished by “strangulation after the Assizes,” but classified as “suspended” (緩決) at the Assizes, resulting in an automatic commutation to exile at 3,000 li. “Strangulation after the Assizes” was also the basic sentence for “mistakenly killing” (誤殺) a bystander while fighting somebody else. Since this crime was committed during a deliberate act of violence, the sentence was adjusted depending on the gravity of the act during which the lethal
