rebind_candidates.anchor_excerpt: Few have paid attention to the ways in which the legal discourse on madness was generated by specific judicial notions, procedures, and concerns. $ ^{3} $ One of the most important tasks of the legal system was to punish criminal acts in proportion of their gravity. Gravity was itself assessed by measuring the degree of intention of the culprit, the kinship proximity between offender and victim, and any other circumstance that officials found relevant. To mete out sentences, legal officials deployed categories such as the “six killings” (11.1) and assessed criminal intention or the relative status of offender and victim. Legal practice had to respect certain procedures (e.g., the necessity to obtain a criminal’s confession before sentencing) and took place within institutional constraints—the annual review of death sentences at the Autumn Assizes, multiple sources of law, the proliferation of ad hoc substitutes, etc. To consider the role of all these factors in the formation of the legal object “madness illness” is to appreciate the inseparability of “the meaning and materiality of law.”

between intentional (gu 故) and non-intentional (sheng 售) acts. $ ^{4} $ This distinction was later preserved in successive codes under different names. $ ^{5} $ Before I explain how Qing homicide laws were structured according to their degree of intention, yet did not rely on the principle of mens rea, let me briefly explain the terms by which Qing law discussed intentions.

The 1667 and 1669 laws concerned only homicides, not madness and mad people in general. The 1669 rule in particular integrated “killing because of madness” within the six killings. Note that in both rate and rationale, the compensation the 1669 rule sought for “killing because of madness” differed significantly from the privilege of monetary redemption granted to some disabled people in statute 22 of the Qing code, which was called “Allowing redemption for the old, the young, and the disabled” (老小廢疾收贖). $ ^{31} $ Under statute 22, one could redeem a death sentence with 0.525 ounces of silver, much less that the 12.42 ounces sought for “killing because of madness.” $ ^{32} $ People who were the object of legalized pity under statute 22 could redeem a real sentence with money; whereas in the case of “[people with] madness illness killing another,” the payment was the sentence, imposed for an inadvertent act that had led to a grave outcome. Compassion was not a factor in the 1669 rule, which likened “killing because of madness” to “accidental killing” on the basis of their common lack of criminal intention. $ ^{33} $

Xu San 徐三, son of Xu Jiuwei 徐九緯, was a “hunter” (打牲人) under the supervision of Palace eunuch Shen Maode 沈茂德. “Hunters” (Manchu: butha; Ch.: buteba 布特哈) was the generic name given to several non-Manchu tribes in an area that is now at the confines of Inner Mongolia and Heilongjiang in northern China. $ ^{23} $ One day with a knife Xu San stabbed (戳) his father, who died shortly thereafter, but before he died, the father had time to testify that “his son had been suffering from madness illness” (伊子原有瘋疾). The Ministry of Punishments approved the sentence that an original memorial (now lost) had proposed: Xu San was exempted of any crime (免罪) because he was judged to have killed “because of madness illness” (因瘋疾). This decision had no other legal repercussions because it was not established as a precedent for judging future cases of a similar nature. $ ^{24} $

of madness” was integrated into the network of the six killings. $ ^{30} $ From then on, and until the end of the Qing, killings committed in a fit of insanity were legally viewed as lacking both awareness and criminal intention.

In imperial Chinese law, a “penal denomination” was not the charge brought against one at the onset of a trial, but the name of the crime for which one was sentenced. Officials selected the fittest “crime name” by assessing the circumstances of the crime, but “[o]nce an offense was judged to fall into a certain category of crime [罪名], the punishment was predetermined.” $ ^{27} $ Both ideally and in practice, the officials who administered this system of fixed punishments were concerned “to maintain a balance between the offense and the penalty.” This judicial approach differed from that of physicians, who gave little importance to denominations in identifying the cause of an illness, and who aimed to put an end to pathological acts rather than find an apt punishment for them.

We should not confuse non-declaration with secrecy. $ ^{119} $ Madness was probably a problem in the treatment of which strangers were not welcome to meddle, but it could hardly be kept secret. People who suddenly turned mad attracted the most attention, as when the friends and relatives of a newly crazy man “filled the hall” (親朋滿座) and asked a visiting physician what could be done. $ ^{120} $ Since wacky speech, wild cursing, and violent fits could hardly go unnoticed in small communities, neighbors and local leaders usually knew when someone suffered from madness. Bai Shengqi was known as “madman Bai” 白瘋子 in surrounding villages even if he had been confined at home for five or six years. $ ^{121} $

Since we still lack an accurate description of how Qing “madness laws” appeared and changed and what kind of object they targeted, our comparison of law with medicine and other Qing fields suffers accordingly. Few have paid attention to the ways in which the legal discourse on madness was generated by specific judicial notions, procedures, and concerns. $ ^{3} $ One of the most important tasks of the legal system was to punish criminal acts in proportion of their gravity. The term xin appeared in expressions like “intentionally” (or “having the intention to”: 有心), “intent upon...” (… 之心) or a combination of both. $ ^{6} $ Yi 意 could be used similarly (as in the phrase “there was initially no intention to harm another” 初無害人之意), but it was also used to describe “unexpected” (出於不意) actions. Yi appeared in the technical phrase “the one who devised the idea [of killing]” (造意者) or in combination with yu 欲 in “there was an intention to kill” (有意欲殺). Gu 故 was used adverbially in “killing intentionally” (故殺). intention was deployed particularly clearly in the network of laws on homicides in which “killing because of madness” would be integrated. The six killings: a hierarchy of intentions Unlike modern Western legal systems, which distinguish between murder and manslaughter or between degrees of homicide, Chinese law in imperial times called all homicides “killings” (sha 殺). Legal commentators customarily divided killings into sub-types. In descending order of gravity, the “Six killings” (六殺) were mou sha 謀殺 (“Killing by design” or “Plotting to kill”), gu sha 故殺 (“Killing intentionally”), dou sha 鬭殺 (“Killing in an affray”), wu sha 誤殺 (“Killing by mistake”), xi sha 戲殺 (“Killing in play”), and guoshi sha 過失殺 (“Killing accidentally”). Three statutes (lǔ 律) sufficed to define killings in the Ming and Qing codes. $ ^{7} $ Here I explain how the “Six killings” applied to the killing of a single person unrelated to the killer. “Killing by design” (謀殺), which was understood as the result of prior scheming (謀) between two or more people, was considered the gravest of the six. $ ^{8} $ The law expected such plots to occur in vendettas, fights over property, or schemes to kill one's own spouse. “The one who devised the idea” (造意者) was to be sentenced to decapitation after the Autumn Assizes (斬監後), where it would be classified as “effective” (情實) even if the main plotter had not taken part in ...

person was what differentiated “killing by design” from “intentional killing” (故殺), which applied when a killer’s homicidal intent was “not known to others” (非人所知). $ ^{10} $ No matter whether this intent to kill was premeditated or arose during a fight (臨時有意欲殺), the lone culprit was judged as having committed “intentional killing” and was sentenced to the same punishment as for “Killing by design”: effective decapitation after the Assizes. “Killing in an affray” (鬪殺) meant killing unintentionally during a fight. $ ^{11} $ In Qing law, intent to kill had become the main difference between “killing in an affray” and “intentional killing.” As a result, those who had killed during a fight had to demonstrate that they had not intended to kill their victim. $ ^{12} $ Under most circumstances, the ensuing sentence of “strangulation after the Assizes” (絞監候) was eventually commuted to the highest sentence below death, namely exile to 3000 li preceded by 100 strokes of the heavy bamboo. In the three remaining killings, which were all discussed in a single statute, death was understood as being inflicted involuntarily. $ ^{13} $ “Killing in play” (戲殺) occurred in the course of dangerous activities in which people engaged purposely, like sword-practice. $ ^{14} $ This rare crime was punished by “strangulation after the Assizes,” but classified as “suspended” (緩決) at the Assizes, resulting ...

mischance had occurred, or, as commentator Shen Zhiqi 沈之奇 specified in the early eighteenth century, on the intention that motivated the act that led to the mistaken killing. $ ^{15} $ “Accidental killing” (過失殺) could be redeemed by conferring 12.42 ounces of silver to the family of the victim to provide for burial fees. $ ^{16} $ This punishment was mild because the law recognized that the culprit had lacked any will to kill, harm, or even partake in dangerous activities. Compensation for a life taken was paid with money, not with one’s own life. Since “Killing because of madness” in the early Qing was sentenced in the same way as “accidental killing,” let me cite the entire text of the relevant statute. $ ^{17} $ Anyone who kills or injures another accidentally $ ^{18} $ (this is comparatively less serious than killing in play) will be sentenced as if for killing or injuring in an affray. [The privilege of] monetary redemption will be granted according to law, and the money will be given to the family (of the person who was killed or injured). (Accident means that which the ear or eye does not extend to, or what was not contemplated. fright, or is driving a cart at a rapid speed on a descent, when the situation makes it so that one cannot stop; $ ^{19} $ or if several persons are carrying a heavy object and the strength [of one] is insufficient to control it so that the other persons carrying it are hurt. The clause specifying that death could not be foreseen pointed to the difference between accidental killing and “Killing in play.” Accidental death was entirely coincidental.
### 11.2 The first Qing laws on madness: creating a new legal object An early case of parricide The earliest Qing case of madness I have identified was a somewhat enigmatic parricide that took place in 1654, about ten years after the Manchus had seized Beijing and officially named it the capital of the newly established ...

because it was sentenced in a way that differed from (as far as I know) all earlier and later cases of parricide in a state of madness in Chinese imperial history, and because it involved a particular class of people that was unique to the early Qing. $ ^{22} $ Xu San 徐三, son of Xu Jiuwei 徐九緯, was a “hunter” (打牲人) under the supervision of Palace eunuch Shen Maode 沈茂德. “Hunters” (Manchu: butha; Ch.: buteba 布特哈) was the generic name given to several non-Manchu tribes in an area that is now at the confines of Inner Mongolia and Heilongjiang in northern China. $ ^{23} $ One day with a knife Xu San stabbed (戳) his father, who died shortly thereafter, but before he died, the father had time to testify that “his son had been suffering from madness illness” (伊子原有瘋疾). The Ministry of Punishments approved the sentence that an original memorial (now lost) had proposed: Xu San was exempted of any crime (免罪) because he was judged to have killed “because of madness illness” (因瘋疾). This decision had no other legal repercussions because it was not established as a precedent for judging future cases of a similar nature. $ ^{24} $ Since this affair concerned tribes under the direct supervision of Manchu institutions like the Imperial Clan and Imperial Household Departments, the memorial reporting the crime may have been written in Manchu before it was translated into Chinese. As I suggested abov ...

Shunzhi-era (1644-1662) memorials were translations from Manchu. The use of feng bing in these Chinese reports may then have pushed the government to adopt feng bing as the official Chinese term for "madness" instead of the more common feng dian 風癲 and feng kuang 風狂, or the more classical diankuang 癲狂 that had been used in Tang and Song law and was still being used in seventeenth-century medicine. A momentous inaccuracy: the 1667 creation of “killing because of madness” The earliest Qing law concerning madness was a terse rule from 1667 mandating that, “Whenever [people with] madness illness kill or injure another, exempt from sentencing” (凡瘋病殺傷人者免議). $ ^{25} $ In its lack of both context and definition, this rule was typical of the “Currently operative regulations of the Ministry of Punishments” (Xingbu xianxing zeli 刑部現行則例) of the Kangxi era (1662-1722), but unlike most Kangxi rules, which usually consisted in precedent-based guidelines for modulating the punishment of existing crimes, the 1667 law created a new penal denomination (罪名): “[people with] madness illness killing another” (瘋病殺人), which I shall render as “killing because of madness.” $ ^{26} $

One influential passage on madness in the Inner Canon has been traced to the earlier Cauterization Canon of the Eleven Yin and Yang Vessels (Yinyang shiyi mai jiu jing 陰陽十一脈灸 經), a work of the late third century BCE that described the disorders that occurred when eleven distinct vessels (脈) “stirred” or “were stirred” (動).⁴ A few lines on the “Yang- Brilliance” (陽明) vessel later found their way into a section of the Basic Questions that later physicians liked to cite when they described kuang.⁵

Pian 49 (“Elucidations on Vessels” 脈解篇) explained: “when the ailment is severe, one desires to mount high and sing, and to cast off clothing and run’ [means that] Yin and Yang [Qi] resume their fight, and they pile on Yang on the outside, $ ^{7} $ making one shed clothes and run” (所謂病至則欲乘高而歌棄衣而走者 陰陽復爭而外並於陽 故使之棄衣而走也; 13.11b). Another gloss in the form of a conversation between the Yellow Emperor and an advisor appeared in pian 30 (“Elucidations on the Yang-Brilliance Vessel” 陽明脈解篇): $ ^{8} $

Pian 49 (“Elucidations on Vessels” 脈解篇) explained: “when the ailment is severe, one desires to mount high and sing, and to cast off clothing and run’ [means that] Yin and Yang [Qi] resume their fight, and they pile on Yang on the outside, $ ^{7} $ making one shed clothes and run” (所謂病至則欲乘高而歌棄衣而走者 陰陽復爭而外並於陽 故使之棄衣而走也; 13.11b). Another gloss in the form of a conversation between the Yellow Emperor and an advisor appeared in pian 30 (“Elucidations on the Yang-Brilliance Vessel” 陽明脈解篇): $ ^{8} $

For the rest of imperial history, medical treatises described kuang with formulaic language derived from chapters 30 and 49 of the Basic Questions, which were not even explicitly concerned with kuang. Only one passage rivaled them in textual dominance—pian 22 (“Dian and kuang” 癲狂篇) of the Divine Pivot, which described “five mental syndromes [that] form a recognizable cluster around the focal concern of kuang” (Chiu 1986: 303). $ ^{9} $

When kuang starts to arise, one is initially dejected and tends to be forgetful, angry, and fearful. This originates in anguish or hunger.... When kuang begins to manifest itself, [one] rests little and one does not feel hungry. One regards oneself as elevated or of superior virtue, as shrewd and wise, or as being highly ranked. One is prone to scolding and yelling, never ceasing day or night.... [Cases of] kuang [that are manifested by] a tendency to be frightened and to laugh, a fondness for songs and music, and wild activity without cease originate in great fear.... [Cases of] kuang [that are manifested by] disorderly vision and hearing and a tendency to call out are produced by insufficient Qi.... [Cases of] kuang [that are manifested by] abundant eating, a tendency to see ghosts and spirits, and a tendency to laugh [to oneself] with no external display originate in having greatly rejoiced at something. $ ^{10} $

Descriptions of madness from the Inner Canon were soon copied into shorter texts, such as the Canon of Problems (Nanjing 難經; a second-century synthesis of the Inner Canon), Zhang Ji's 張機 Treatise on Cold Injury and Miscellaneous Illnesses (Shanghan zabing lun 傷寒雜病論; ca. 200 CE), Huangfu Mi's 皇甫謐 AB Canon of Acumoxa (Zhenjiu jiayijing 針灸甲乙經; 256/262), and Wang Xi's 王熙 Canon of the Pulse (Maijing 脈經; third century).¹¹ The most commonly cited in later imperial times was the Canon of Problems.¹² "How are the illnesses kuang and dian to be distinguished?" (狂癲之病，何以別之), inquired the 59th problem.

page_contexts.ocr_excerpt: task. Yet case records remind us that illness meanings were more than organic. As an affliction that was defined socially, insanity was always more than a bodily event. $ ^{6} $ Categories and scripts channeled phenomena into intelligible and manageable forms; they also guided perception and suggested methods to tackle the reality they purported to describe. Broadly speaking, medical specialists attributed mad symptoms either to invasion by external forces (“deviant Qi”, Wind, ghosts) or to internal imbalances strong enough to provoke illness. $ ^{7} $ Doctors claimed they could cure their patients by expelling these external invaders or redressing these pathogenic imbalances. Because of this emphasis on dynamic situations and a general therapeutic optimism, the medical tradition considered permanent disorders anomalous. Chinese physicians therefore rarely medicalized personality and almost never called patients “madmen” (狂人 or 瘋子). The medical scripts by which doctors understood madness changed significantly between the eighth and the fourteenth century, and again from the fourteenth to the sixteenth century. I plan to demonstrate that new explanations and treatments for insanity often borrowed from the prototypical scenarios of other illnesses or from contemporaneous interpretations of human vitality rather than from the observation of mad patients. The second half of this chapter traces how the growing prominence of mucus as a cause of illness starting in the eleventh century deeply altered the way in which Chinese physicians understood and handled madness. I first describe the origins of the notion of “mucus,” a pathological fluid that was not mentioned in the Inner Canon. I then review how the medical activism and publishing activities of the northern-Song government (976-1127) helped to create a pool of common references that facilitated medical debates and intertextual borrowings. For example, many Song doctors debated the claim made in a government-sponsored formulary that “Wind strokes” could be treated with vomit-inducing (“emetic”) drugs. Just around the same time (the turn of the twelfth century), these mucus-targeting emetics were starting to be used to treat madness. ### 3.1 The kuang 狂 core in the Yellow Emperor corpus Modern scholars agree that the Yellow Emperor's Inner Canon (Huangdi neijing 黄帝内經) was "an unintegrated compilation of short writings" (Sivin 1995f: 191). Its received versions—the Grand Basis, the Basic Questions, and the Divine Pivot, all of which date to the seventh century or later but were compiled from sources as early as the third century BCE²—were "simply different collections of similar material. Any one collection therefore represented the end of one lineage of collecting, as if frozen ...

explanations and doctrinal statements. $ ^{3} $ Elite physicians from the eleventh to the twentieth century proclaimed it should form the core of medical education. The diversity of views contained in the Canon allowed a number of rival doctrines hinging on it to claim authenticity. One influential passage on madness in the Inner Canon has been traced to the earlier Cauterization Canon of the Eleven Yin and Yang Vessels (Yinyang shiyi mai jiu jing 陰陽十一脈灸 經), a work of the late third century BCE that described the disorders that occurred when eleven distinct vessels (脈) “stirred” or “were stirred” (動).⁴ A few lines on the “Yang- Brilliance” (陽明) vessel later found their way into a section of the Basic Questions that later physicians liked to cite when they described kuang.⁵ When this [vessel] is moved, one ails from: shivers and ailing from coldness; fondness for stretching; continual yawning, blackness in the center of the forehead; ailing from swelling; when severe, detestation for people and fire; when hearing the sound of wood, panicked alarm; panicky heart; the desire to close doors and windows and dwell in isolation; when the ailment is severe, the desire to mount high and sing, and to cast off clothing and run. These constitute shin reversal. For these [ailments], the Yang-Brilliance vessel controls the treatment.

Zhang Ji's Treatise on Cold Injury (ca. 200 CE) is the earliest extant work proposing to treat psycho-behavioral symptoms with drug-based remedies. $ ^{13} $ It generally attributed fits of madness (發狂)—discussed in a section on Yang-brilliance disorders—to excessive Heat. Zhang Ji was probably describing the ability of high fever to create dementia. The symptoms were ghostly visions (also mentioned in Divine Pivot 22 and the Canon of the Pulse), wild language (妄語; Basic Questions 30), and ceaseless wild movements (妄行不休: Divine Pivot 22 and Canon of Problems 59). For fits of madness, the Treatise proposed to “make Blood descend” (下血); it prescribed “Decoction of resistance” (抵當湯) to treat “as if manic” cases (如狂, less severe than fits). For serious disorders of the Yang-brilliance vessel, with madness or possession symptoms, the “Great Qi-supporting decoction” (大承氣湯) was recommended. These recipes were all purgatives designed to expel the inner Heat that was thought to cause the symptoms. The Great Qi-supporting decoction later became an important remedy for madness (see 4.1, 4.2). Until the nineteenth century, physicians described the core symptoms of kuang with language borrowed from the Inner Canon and its shorter derivative works, adopting in passing the notion that madness mostly meant unusual behavior. I noted in Chapter 1 that Martha Li Chiu (1986) scoured the Inner Canon to find a Chinese medical object comparable to mental illness, and that she settled on kuang because it was mostly characterized by "mental symptoms." But by describing symptoms (rather than illnesses) as either mental or physical, she reproduced a problematic dichotomy at a lower level without uncovering the assumptions it hides. Below, I propose an alternative way of comparing Chinese medical approaches with psychiatric ones. My goal is to foster our understanding of medical discourse on kuang by breaking free from the preconceptions that lurk beneath the notions of "mental" and "physical." ### 3.2 Psycho-behavioral pathology in Chinese medicine Comparisons: why not “mental illness”? Most scholars (Chinese and Western alike) who have written on insanity in Chinese history since the 1950s have used the phrase “mental illness” either to describe their object or to pose this notion as the basis for comparing psychiatry with Chinese medicine. $ ^{14} $ But the pure notion of “mental illness” with which Chinese conceptions are so often contrasted (or deemed incomparable) may never have existed. From the era of “mad-doctoring” to the latest edition of the Diagnostic and Statistical Manual of Mental Disorders (or DSM; see APA 1994), behavioral symptoms have been at the center of psychiatric attention. unmatched_anchors.source_text: In both etiology and treatment, extant cases of “mad women” predating 1500 are indistinguishable from those of men. Xu Shuwei 許叔微 (1079-1154) gave the same pill to a woman who had been scared out of her wits by pirates and to a man who had lost his mind as he was about to be executed as a criminal. Wang Haogu 王好古 (13 $ ^{th} $ century) cured a manic woman with a purgative he borrowed from the Treatise on Cold Injury. $ ^{4} $ Two cases by Ni Weide 倪維德 (1303-1377) displayed the same gender neutrality. $ ^{5} $ If these physicians made distinctions between genders, they did not record them in their case files.

Even one of the best-known Song works on women's illnesses proposed solutions that were equally applicable to men. Chen Ziming's 陳自明 All-Inclusive Excellent Recipes for Women (Furen daquan liangfang 婦人大全良方; hereafter Excellent Recipes), which he compiled in the mid thirteenth century, regrouped a large number of recipes designed to treat disorders as they happened to women. $ ^{6} $ On the issue that concerns us, Chen maintained that, "In women, the disorders dian and kuang are brought about by the deviant [Qi] of Wind, which is absorbed when Qi and Blood are depleted" (夫婦人顛狂病者 由血氣虛受風邪所為也). $ ^{7} $ Despite the mention of Blood depletion, this etiology was not gendered, because it corresponded with the traditional etiology of most Wind disorders. $ ^{8} $

In a brief case record, Chen explained that he cured a woman with the “Elixir that nourishes the correct” (養正丹), a common remedy for madness and seizures. $ ^{9} $ But as if to emphasize that this treatment was not designed exclusively for women, Chen mentioned that he also cured a man with the same remedy. Despite the popularity of Chen’s Excellent Recipes after Xue Ji 薛己 (1487-1559) re-edited them in 1547, there is no sign that Chen’s explanation of women’s mad behavior was ever adopted by elite physicians of the sixteenth century and after, probably because the Wind explanation that Chen had embraced had by then been replaced by a script that traced most problems involving consciousness to the disorderly movements of Fire and mucus in and around the Heart. $ ^{10} $

A few sixteenth-century cases tell us what therapies were current at the time. The physicians who designed them did not seem to think these remedies applied to women better than to men. Wu Qiu 吳球 (fl. 1520s-30s) traced a young woman's strange behavior (she "would cry or laugh, or run around naked, or shut the doors and speak a lot" (或哭或笑 或裸體而走 或閉戶而多言) to "harm caused by excessive ratiocination, [which made] the spirit unable to hold to its lodging" (思慮過傷 神不守舍也). $ ^{11} $ In the sixteenth century, this etiology typified medicine's increasing focus on "inner harm"—a category that included exhaustion, food scarcity or excesses, and emotional imbalances—and the devising of new ways to treat it. rebind_candidates.anchor_excerpt: A woman who has a fit of dian does not know shame: she becomes candy-like $ ^{46} $ when she sees a man and very angry when she sees a woman. She will go as far as going naked and exposing her body. In such situations, Liver Fire is ardent and [the woman] thinks about a man but is unable to get one: this causes stasis and congealing that turn into dian. How does ardent Liver Fire turn into dian? It must be because, in women, Liver-Wood is flourishing: $ ^{47} $ Liver Fire coerces the Heart, causing the Heart’s ruler to descend from the palace. But the Heart’s Envelope keeps guard on the outside, so how can it let [the Heart, i.e. the ruler] be intimidated? $ ^{48} $ It is little known that Liver Fire is none other than depletion Fire. $ ^{49} $ Depletion Fire and Ministerial Fire are of the same sort. When [Fire?] associates with brigands, $ ^{50} $ it forgets [the ruler’s] sagely brilliance $ ^{51} $ and it directly sets the palace ablaze. But once the Heart-ruler walks out, why is there only dian and not death? This must be because Kidney Water comes to the rescue. Thinking of a man and being unable to get him is because the Kidneys are flourishing. Although this is Kidney Fire, Kidney Water is actually exhausted. When Liver Fire becomes coercive, the Heart [should be] provided for by Kidney Water: this is why there is only dian and not death. The treatment method is to purge the Liver root and replenish Kidney Water, as well as to channel static Qi. $ ^{52} $

rebind_candidates.anchor_excerpt: Several cases by Gong Tingxian 龔廷賢 (fl. 1570s-1610s) indicate that one physician could propose many different explanations for similar behaviors, an aspect of late-imperial medicine with which we are now familiar. In two records published in 1576 and 1587, Gong treated female patients with the “Avert-wind tongsheng powder” 防風通聖散, a formula that a book by his father had claimed could “treat the suddenly erupting symptoms of all madness and Wind disorders” (治一切癲狂風疾 暴發之症).¹⁸ When he re-edited his father’s work, Gong added that this remedy “treats diankuang that has just started to arise” (此方治癲狂初起), when “in most cases there is repletion-Heat and Wind-heteropathy” (多有實熱風邪).¹⁹ In later books, Gong explained that women’s dian behavior (singing at strange times and “climbing on rooftops” 歌唱無時 簫垣上屋) was “caused by constructive Blood (?) lost in the Heart’s Envelope” (營血迷於心包所致也).²⁰ I do not know what Gong Tingxian meant by “constructive Blood,” but it may have been unique to women. As we have seen, however, blockage of the “Heart’s Envelope” was one of the most commonly identified causes of disorders of consciousness for both men and women in late-

I shall agree with Furth in general, but I will also show that the etiology of madness was already ungendered in the Song dynasty (960-1279), and that glimpses of gendered etiologies only appeared in late-imperial times, especially in the nineteenth century. In recorded symptoms, identified etiology, and adopted therapy, a spectrum of more or less gendered solutions were available. Some symptoms (notably nudity and grief) were proportionally more common in women's cases, probably because these acts were seen as socially more problematic, and were therefore more likely to be brought to the attention of a physician. But although many nineteenth-century cases of mad women did not conform to what treatises said about insanity, most of them did. $ ^{3} $

page_contexts.ocr_excerpt: whether postpartum insanity was “really madness,” since late-imperial physicians tied it explicitly to what they called madness. ### 9.1 Non-gendered bodies, gendered etiologies Medical explanations of mad behavior in treatises purported to apply to both men and women. But in practice, did doctors consistently trace cases of women's mad behavior to the roots they identified in their treatises? Did their descriptions and treatments apply to the "androgynous body" (Furth 1999: 25-29, 44-48 and 56-57)—a body without anatomical sex—that characterized elite Chinese medicine? Charlotte Furth has indeed argued that late-imperial physicians focused increasingly less on the uniqueness of women, and tended to understand the women's illnesses in the same way as they understood men's. unmatched_anchors.source_text: In this chapter, I discuss the ways in which physicians explained and handled symptoms that resembled those of involuntary possession. $ ^{3} $ By possession, I refer to invasion by a non-human (or no longer human) force that could supposedly control the thoughts, acts, and speech of their victim. Possession was expressed by verbs like ping 憑, yi 依, or fu 附, which meant “attaching to” or “relying upon” a person. Ghosts were also often said to “enter” (ru 入) the body of their victims. All these terms were not part of specialized medical terminology, but physicians used them when they discussed cases of “possession.”

Zhu Zhenheng's 朱震亨 (1282-1358) influential view that possession symptoms were commonly triggered by mucus. $ ^{7} $ The third section tries to interpret the dramatic decrease in mentions of exorcistic healers in late-imperial cases that physicians or their patients' relatives explicitly attributed to "evil ghosts" or designated as "spectral affliction." I suggest that shamans and elite physicians no longer competed for the same patients. Yet, as I show in the fourth section, even in the late nineteenth century many elite physicians still ascribed illness to the action of various kinds of ghosts. The "medical exorcisms" they performed often borrowed from ritual therapies that today we associate more readily with Daoism than with scholarly medicine. $ ^{8} $ The final section explains how some physicians explained and treated hallucinations, a special type of possession symptom.

Medical texts expressed the similarity between the symptoms of possession and those of medical madness in several ways. Many late-imperial physicians discussed “spectral affliction” under the same treatise entry as insanity. The second half of the madness section in Li Chan’s 李梴 Introduction to Medical Learning (Yixue rumen 醫學入門, 1575), for instance, analyzed the origin of “afflictions by evil” (邪祟). $ ^{12} $ Others doctors proposed explicit similes, as when Zhang Lu 張璐 (1615-1699) noted that one of his insane patients looked “as if he were possessed by a specter” (似祟憑依) or when Gong Tingxian 龔廷賢 (ca. 1538-1635) claimed that one vaunted recipe “treats fits of madness in which one jumps and screams as though one had incurred a demonic affliction” (治發狂跳叫如著了鬼祟一般者). $ ^{13} $ Li

Many late-imperial physicians discussed “spectral affliction” under the same treatise entry as insanity. The second half of the madness section in Li Chan’s 李梴 Introduction to Medical Learning (Yixue rumen 醫學入門, 1575), for instance, analyzed the origin of “afflictions by evil” (邪祟). $ ^{12} $ Others doctors proposed explicit similes, as when Zhang Lu 張璐 (1615-1699) noted that one of his insane patients looked “as if he were possessed by a specter” (似祟憑依) or when Gong Tingxian 龔廷賢 (ca. 1538-1635) claimed that one vaunted recipe “treats fits of madness in which one jumps and screams as though one had incurred a demonic affliction” (治發狂跳叫如著了鬼祟一般者). $ ^{13} $ Li

could treat. $ ^{9} $ Some passages warned that many kinds of spirits could make people crazy. $ ^{10} $ Others called “mad words and blithe speech” kinds of “spectral afflictions” (狂言妄语… 此等之祟) and said that “specters with a form… render people mad” (諸有形之祟…令人 顛狂). $ ^{11} $ If these texts did not assert that madness was always caused by demons, at least they implied that specters could cause some cases of mad behavior. What is crucial is that religious, medical, and legal texts alike could use terms like diankuang to refer a similar set of symptoms.

Descriptions of possession also appeared in the madness sections of Zhang Siwei’s Yimen mizhu 醫門秘旨 (ca. 1576; 6.19a; RBXC: 703) and Li Zhongzi’s Bingji shazhuan 病機沙篆 (1667; juan 2, sec. 6, “Madness” 癲狂; Li ZhYQ: 468B). An entry in the madness section of the anonymous Yifang biannan dacheng 醫方辨難大成 (1850) was devoted to possession (juan 92, “Diankuang biannan” 顛狂辨難, “Are some manic symptoms caused by harm done by evil spirits?” 狂證果有邪祟為害否; 2005 [1850]: 534-35).

rebind_candidates.anchor_excerpt: Regardless of whether they believed in ghosts, most scholarly healers included sections on “demonic affliction” (鬼祟), “affliction by evil” (邪祟), “spectral illnesses” (祟病), or “evil demons” (邪鬼) in their treatises or case collections. Ancient texts had attributed all kinds of ailments to spirits, from toothaches to painful childbirth. $ ^{4} $ Most late-imperial doctors, however, attributed to ghostly possession chiefly a cluster of symptoms that looked like those of madness. These “possession symptoms” included delirium, strange speech, unprovoked grief, extreme apathy, hallucinations and the desire to injure oneself.

Even one of the best-known Song works on women's illnesses proposed solutions that were equally applicable to men. Chen Ziming's 陳自明 All-Inclusive Excellent Recipes for Women (Furen daquan liangfang 婦人大全良方; hereafter Excellent Recipes), which he compiled in the mid thirteenth century, regrouped a large number of recipes designed to treat disorders as they happened to women. $ ^{6} $ On the issue that concerns us, Chen maintained that, "In women, the disorders dian and kuang are brought about by the deviant [Qi] of Wind, which is absorbed when Qi and Blood are depleted" (夫婦人顛狂病者 由血氣虛受風邪所為也). $ ^{7} $ Despite the mention of Blood depletion, this etiology was not gendered, because it corresponded with the traditional etiology of most Wind disorders. $ ^{8} $

governor-general, who, after another review (usually made by his legal secretaries), transcribed all the evidence into a tiben 題本, a “routine memorial” that he sent to the Grand Secretariat (Neige 內閣), which forwarded it to the Ministry of Punishments for final confirmation. $ ^{6} $ In a new memorial, the “Office of Scrutiny of the Ministry of Punishments” (Xingke 刑科) sometimes rejected (bo 駁), but usually confirmed the sentence proposed by the governor. $ ^{7} $ These “routine memorials” recounted entire incidents—from before the killing to the arrest—and the way they were processed by the judicial system. They even cited depositions from physicians or the relatives of mad killers, and even from the very men who were under trial. $ ^{8} $ Much of Chapters 13 to 15 are based on these depositions.

page_contexts.ocr_excerpt: In this chapter, I discuss the ways in which physicians explained and handled symptoms that resembled those of involuntary possession. $ ^{3} $ By possession, I refer to invasion by a non-human (or no longer human) force that could supposedly control the thoughts, acts, and speech of their victim. Possession was expressed by verbs like ping 憑, yi 依, or fu 附, which meant “attaching to” or “relying upon” a person. Ghosts were also often said to “enter” (ru 入) the body of their victims. All these terms were not part of specialized medical terminology, but physicians used them when they discussed cases of “possession.” Regardless of whether they believed in ghosts, most scholarly healers included sections on “demonic affliction” (鬼祟), “affliction by evil” (邪祟), “spectral illnesses” (祟病), or “evil demons” (邪鬼) in their treatises or case collections. Ancient texts had attributed all kinds of ailments to spirits, from toothaches to painful childbirth. $ ^{4} $ Most late-imperial doctors, however, attributed to ghostly possession chiefly a cluster of symptoms that looked like those of madness. These “possession symptoms” included delirium, strange speech, unprovoked grief, extreme apathy, hallucinations and the desire to injure oneself. The concept of sui 崇 is almost as old as the Chinese writing system itself. Donald Harper has glossed sui (in ancient usage) as a calamity brought abou ...

Zhu Zhenheng's 朱震亨 (1282-1358) influential view that possession symptoms were commonly triggered by mucus. $ ^{7} $ The third section tries to interpret the dramatic decrease in mentions of exorcistic healers in late-imperial cases that physicians or their patients' relatives explicitly attributed to "evil ghosts" or designated as "spectral affliction." I suggest that shamans and elite physicians no longer competed for the same patients. Yet, as I show in the fourth section, even in the late nineteenth century many elite physicians still ascribed illness to the action of various kinds of ghosts. The "medical exorcisms" they performed often borrowed from ritual therapies that today we associate more readily with Daoism than with scholarly medicine. $ ^{8} $ The final section explains how some physicians explained and treated hallucinations, a special type of possession symptom. ### 8.2 The ambiguous closeness of madness and possession symptoms People from the times and places I study often found resemblances between what they called dian or kuang and what they called “spectral affliction” (sui). These categories were not equivalent, but both specialists and commoners found them meaningfully related in a number of contexts. First, acts designated as “mad” could be attributed to possession, because the terms that meant “madness” did not necessarily entail a medical explanation. Ev ...

could treat. $ ^{9} $ Some passages warned that many kinds of spirits could make people crazy. $ ^{10} $ Others called “mad words and blithe speech” kinds of “spectral afflictions” (狂言妄语… 此等之祟) and said that “specters with a form… render people mad” (諸有形之祟…令人 顛狂). $ ^{11} $ If these texts did not assert that madness was always caused by demons, at least they implied that specters could cause some cases of mad behavior. What is crucial is that religious, medical, and legal texts alike could use terms like diankuang to refer a similar set of symptoms. Medical texts expressed the similarity between the symptoms of possession and those of medical madness in several ways. Many late-imperial physicians discussed “spectral affliction” under the same treatise entry as insanity. The second half of the madness section in Li Chan’s 李梴 Introduction to Medical Learning (Yixue rumen 醫學入門, 1575), for instance, analyzed the origin of “afflictions by evil” (邪祟). $ ^{12} $ Others doctors proposed explicit similes, as when Zhang Lu 張璐 (1615-1699) noted that one of his insane patients looked “as if he were possessed by a specter” (似祟憑依) or when Gong Tingxian 龔廷賢 (ca. 1538-1635) claimed that one vaunted recipe “treats fits of madness in which one jumps and screams as though one had incurred a demonic affliction” (治發狂跳叫如著了鬼祟一般者). $ ^{13} $ Li

Yongcui 李用粹 wrote an entry called “Madness resembles spectral affliction” (癫狂似祟) in his Collected Supplements on Symptoms and Treatments (Zhengzhi buibu 證治匯補; 1687). Another sign of the ambiguous meaning of possession symptoms was that many physicians treated as the result of organic imbalances symptoms that patients attributed to possession. $ ^{14} $ In non-medical contexts, mad behavior was often understood and handled as if it resulted from possession. When Lin Shiyuan started to “speak and dance madly” (狂言跳舞) in 1762, his mother thought he had been “struck by evil” (中邪); she sent a relative to invite a Taoist master (道師) to chant scriptures and implore deities (誦經祇禳) on his behalf. $ ^{15} $ The Kangxi emperor (r. 1662-1722) was so perplexed by his son Yinren’s reckless behavior that he first attributed it to sorcery and evil spirits. $ ^{16} $ When in 1713 he decided to remove Yinren as heir-apparent after decades of trouble, he had resigned himself that his son’s madness was an illness (瘋狂成疾 and 狂疾) and that Yinren would never recover. $ ^{17} $ The attribution of madness to possession seemed natural in literature as well, as when the son of a corrupt official in a story by Yuan Mei 袁枚 (1716-1797) was said to have “suddenly caught madness” (忽得瘋疾) and it later turned out that the young man was being possessed. $ ^{18} $

###### 8.3 Possession... or mucus? Considering the resemblance between the symptoms of mania and possession, it is not surprising that physicians devised ways to strengthen their interpretive claims on such symptoms. By arguing that disorders that looked as if they were caused by demons could in fact be caused by either mucus or depletion, Zhu Zhenheng 朱震亨 (1282-1358) managed to discuss possession symptoms without discussing ghosts (4.4). Zhu's argument started its rise to textual dominance in the writings of his disciples and intellectual followers. The sections on "mucus" and "depletion and wasting disorders" (虚損) of Liu Chun's Yuji weiji 玉機微義 (1396) discussed the idea that mucus caused possession-like symptoms. $ ^{19} $ Dai Liang's "Biography of the Old Man of Cinnabar Creek" ("Danxi weng zhuan" 丹溪翁傳), which contained one of the mucus-possession cases I translated in 4.4, was appended to the widely circulated Huizhou version of the Danxi xinfā 丹溪心法 (1482). Wang Ao's 王鏊 Gu danfang 古單方 (completed in 1496) contained the earliest section on "affliction by evil" that I have found. $ ^{20} $ In it, Wang acknowledged Zhu's view that the symptoms of such afflictions could indeed be caused by mucus, and cited Zhu's warning that those afflicted by mucus possession might die if they were treated by exorcism. At the beginning of their sections on spectral afflictions, many late-imperia ...

虞搏 Yixue zhengzhuan 醫學正傳 (1515; 5.7), the composite Danxi xiansheng zhifa xinyao 丹溪先生治法心要 (1536; 5.5), Li Chan's Introduction to Medical Learning (1575), Zhang Siwei's 張四維 Yimen mizhi 醫門秘旨 (ca. 1576), Zhang Sanxi's 張三錫 Yixue liuyao 醫學六要 (1585), and most of Gong Tingxian's popular books (7.3) probably enhanced the dominance of Zhu's idea in medical circles. $ ^{21} $ The claim that mucus could create strange disorders had thus became part of mainstream medical discourse by the sixteenth century, and it became such commonsense in the Qing period that it no longer needed attribution. An Anthology of Strange Illnesses (Qizheng bui 奇症匯) compiled in 1786 recounted the case of a man who saw lions in front of him. Without further remarks his illness was attributed to mucus and treated with emetics. $ ^{22} $ About a case of “demonic infestation” (鬼疰) included in his Medical Case Files Ancient and Present, With Commentaries (Guijin yian an 古今醫案按; 1778), Yu Zhen remarked that “This is what is called ‘mucus [causes] many strange symptoms’; it is not something to be marveled at” (此所謂痰多怪證亦不為奇). $ ^{23} $ He commented elsewhere that the commonest treatment for possession-like symptoms in his time was “clearing Heat and guiding mucus” (清熱導痰). $ ^{24} $ Medicine for Sots (Chunzi yi 蠢子醫), a book that one man wrote for his grandchildren in the late nineteenth century, observed twice that the mai ...

disorders was to attack mucus: “when mucus and saliva make a demonic affliction and Cold and Heat go back and forth, the methods of guiding or vomiting [mucus] will be the only ones to work” (痰涎為祟 寒熱往來 非用導吐法不行). $ ^{25} $ ### 8.4 Diminished competition between physicians and shamans? Even if Zhu Zhenheng was never part of an organized campaign to root out popular practices and did not speak in the name of a profession, he clearly aimed to reject exorcism as a treatment for possession symptoms. His understanding of body and illness disqualified shamanic healers to the benefit of people who—like himself—claimed they could reestablish organic balance with herbal or mineral drugs. Though there is no reason to doubt that he was genuinely concerned with his patients' well-being, his interpretation of possession symptoms can also be understood as a move in a competition with other kinds of healers. Ascribing mad symptoms to mucus was a way of saying they were not caused by ghosts and thus of invalidating any recourse to ritual specialists. One of Zhu's cases explicitly mentioned this contest between healers. $ ^{26} $ An adult woman surnamed Jin. During the hot summer months, she went to a banquet. When she returned, her mother-in-law reprimanded her for not sitting at the proper place. She then felt so ashamed that she contracted this illness. In the midst of incoherent words, she of ...

Almost all the Jin and Yuan physicians who recorded madness cases alluded to this rivalry. Zhang Congzheng 張從正 (1156-1228) explained that the relatives of a man had burned paper talismans (燒符), organized Taoist rituals (作醮), and hired a dancing medium to exorcise the ghost (問鬼跳誣) they thought had crazed him after he had fallen off a horse. $ ^{27} $ Wang Haogu 王好古 (fl. first half of the 13 $ ^{th} $ century) noted that the relatives of a woman who had become crazy “all thought it was a ghost’s making” (人皆以為鬼魔). $ ^{28} $ His younger contemporary Luo Tianyi 羅天益 (ca. 1220-1290) similarly reported that people around his mad patient all said that Wind demons were causing the problem (時人皆言風魔了), though the entreaties of an exorcistic master (巫師) had failed to cure him. $ ^{29} $ Whereas most Jin-Yuan physicians invited to treat mad behavior noted that their patients had previously sought the help of shamanic healers, Ming and Qing doctors almost never mentioned exorcists as their competitors. A complete lack of medical case files between the late fourteenth century and approximately 1515 makes me unable to follow this trend in the documents. All we see is an end result: after 1500, demonology was no longer the most criticized approach to mad symptoms in medical treatises and case collections. Instead, medical writers almost always contrasted their own approach with that of other phys ...

unmatched_anchors.source_text: The seriousness of crimes was defined comparatively by the main statutes (律) of the Code, which were supposed to form a well-balanced network of crimes and punishments. The Code also provided a table of “mourning degrees” according to which the gravity of crimes against relatives was assessed. $ ^{4} $ As Table 12.1 (next page) illustrates, the order of severity of the six killings was preserved even after the degree of family relation between killer and victim had been factored in. In the table, the punishment increases downwards (toward more serious kinds of homicides) and toward the right (from junior relatives to senior relatives as victims). The middle column defines the standard punishment for the killing of a single unrelated person (凡人). The punishments meted out therefore reflected not only the nature of the act itself, but also the relative status of offender and victim. $ ^{5} $ In the terms of our table, the hierarchy of the punishments imposed for proximate crimes was thus observed both vertically and horizontally.

In trials, it was an official's duty to select the “penal denomination” (罪名) that most appropriately described an unlawful act. The sentencing of a crime was called kezui 科罪 or duanzui 斷罪, in which zui referred both to the crime as defined by the Code, and to the penalty that this crime legally demanded. $ ^{6} $ Many legal articles and countless manuals for

magistrates instructed officials on how to judge cases. $ ^{8} $ The selection of the most fitting crime name was based on a careful assessment of the circumstances of the crime, including the degree of intention involved, the relative status of offender and victim, and anything else that could be relevant—conflict over property, particular cruelty, the number of times one had hit a victim, etc. For “circumstances” (情) to be balanced with “law” (法 or 律), “light” (輕) crimes had to be punished lightly, and “severe” (重) crimes sentenced severely. Officials who selected the wrong penal denomination or punished either too severely or too lightly could face administrative sanctions. $ ^{9} $

For almost a hundred years after 1669, homicides committed in a fit of madness were sentenced as “killing because of madness” regardless of the identity and number of the victims, because the law considered that the lack of intention of mad acts justified a light punishment. $ ^{11} $ Starting around the mid-eighteenth century, however, a few concerned officials argued that treating in bulk all killings committed because of madness created an imbalance between some crimes and their punishments. The acts of legislation we are about to examine did not stem from new reflections on madness, but from efforts to reestablish consistent and well-balanced penalties for homicides committed because of madness.

In the second half of the eighteenth century, two important phenomena enabled madness laws to proliferate and become more severe. One was the general proliferation of sub-statutes, which after 1740 could be cited in judgments without reference to statutes. $ ^{12} $ Second was the subsequent development of the sentencing guidelines found in “completed cases” (成案) and in the regulations of the Autumn Assizes. $ ^{13} $ Both events helped to modulate in a formal way the punishments that mad killers incurred depending on whom they had killed.

In the second half of the eighteenth century, two important phenomena enabled madness laws to proliferate and become more severe. One was the general proliferation of sub-statutes, which after 1740 could be cited in judgments without reference to statutes. $ ^{12} $ Second was the subsequent development of the sentencing guidelines found in “completed cases” (成案) and in the regulations of the Autumn Assizes. $ ^{13} $ Both events helped to modulate in a formal way the punishments that mad killers incurred depending on whom they had killed.

Legislative developments stemmed from the initiatives of the emperor, provincial judges and governors, and officials from the Ministry of Punishments. The more highly ranked the proponents of change, the more likely new measures were to be implemented. It was usually the Ministry of Punishments that decided whether to turn such proposals into laws. Eighteenth-century changes may thus have stemmed from the habitus of the Ministry of Punishments and the provincial bureaucracy under the Qianlong reign (1736-1796), a topic we know almost nothing about. $ ^{14} $

The earliest example of a desire to adjust punishments for crimes committed “because of madness” was atypically systematic. In 1754, Shanxi provincial judge Jiang Zhou 蔣洲 (d. 1760) memorialized to “balance the sub-statute on killing because of madness” (請平瘋病殺人之例). $ ^{15} $ He first noted that those who had killed an older brother in a fit of madness had been sentenced in two conflicting ways: 1) to 3 years of penal servitude and 100 strokes of the heavy stick for the “Accidental killing of a superior or senior relative of the second degree” (過失殺期親尊長), $ ^{16} $ or 2) to “immediate decapitation” (斬立決) according to the statute on “killing one’s elder brother,” $ ^{17} $ but with an inserted “slip” 簽 declaring that the killer had been mad and that his sentence should therefore be commuted to “decapitation after the Assizes” (斬監侯). $ ^{18} $ The first kind of judgment reasserted the likening of mad homicides with accidental killings that had founded the 1669 law on “killing because of madness,” whereas the second way of sentencing treated madness only as a

The Code also provided a table of “mourning degrees” according to which the gravity of crimes against relatives was assessed. $ ^{4} $ As Table 12.1 (next page) illustrates, the order of severity of the six killings was preserved even after the degree of family relation between killer and victim had been factored in. In the table, the punishment increases downwards (toward more serious kinds of homicides) and toward the right (from junior relatives to senior relatives as victims). The middle column defines the standard punishment for the killing of a single unrelated person (凡人). The punishments meted out therefore reflected not only the nature of the act itself, but also the relative status of offender and victim. $ ^{5} $ In the terms of our table, the hierarchy of the punishments imposed for proximate crimes was thus observed both vertically and horizontally.

or to one that imposed a punishment that was deemed appropriate to the crime at hand. $ ^{10} $ Thanks to analogies, the judicial system could sentence a potentially unlimited number of criminal acts with a limited number of laws. But judicial analogies could only be deployed when no law was found relevant to the punishment of a specific act. This strictly regulated process differed from what doctors did when they treated an illness by analogy to their understanding of another disorder. In Part One, I argued that this kind of “scheme transfer” was the main way in which medical approaches to madness changed.

The earliest example of a desire to adjust punishments for crimes committed “because of madness” was atypically systematic. In 1754, Shanxi provincial judge Jiang Zhou 蔣洲 (d. 1760) memorialized to “balance the sub-statute on killing because of madness” (請平瘋病殺人之例). $ ^{15} $ He first noted that those who had killed an older brother in a fit of madness had been sentenced in two conflicting ways: 1) to 3 years of penal servitude and 100 strokes of the heavy stick for the “Accidental killing of a superior or senior relative of the second degree” (過失殺期親尊長), $ ^{16} $ or 2) to “immediate decapitation” (斬立決) according to the statute on “killing one’s elder brother,” $ ^{17} $ but with an inserted “slip” 簽 declaring that the killer had been mad and that his sentence should therefore be commuted to “decapitation after the Assizes” (斬監侯). $ ^{18} $ The first kind of judgment reasserted the likening of mad homicides with accidental killings that had founded the 1669 law on “killing because of madness,” whereas the second way of sentencing treated madness only as a

In 1784, the Shandong governor sentenced to permanent imprisonment a man who had slain a fourth-degree uncle during a fit of madness, but the Ministry of Punishments overturned his decision. $ ^{33} $ Explaining that the sub-statute on “killing because of madness” only applied to the killing of a single unrelated person—this was a new distinction—the Ministry decreed that all killings of senior relatives committed in a fit of insanity should now be sentenced according to the root statutes (本律) on the homicide of senior relatives. $ ^{34} $ From then on, the identity of the victim, not the presence of madness, would determine the choice of penal denomination. The Ministry’s decision became law in 1786.

The second group of mad killers that judicial officials encountered was those who never recovered from madness after committing homicide. Chronic delirium kept a killer from providing the confession that was necessary for a sentence to be pronounced. When such cases occurred, legal reports almost invariably noted that the killer's "eyes were inexpressive and his words incoherent: no confession can be extracted" (两目直瞪 語無論次 不能取供). $ ^{61} $ People like this were considered "permanently crazy" (始終瘋迷). When they were not executed for a serious crime like parricide, they were sentenced to permanent imprisonment.
Caizuo 李裁左, who had killed his paternal uncle in 1889, was submitted to torture but he showed no fear (加以刑嚇 罔知畏懼). $ ^{63} $

It is more difficult to find social roots for transformations in the legal treatment of insane killers. I try to show instead that the dynamics of madness as a legal object was governed by the way the Qing legal system functioned. In the second half of the eighteenth century, two important phenomena enabled madness laws to proliferate and become more severe. One was the general proliferation of sub-statutes, which after 1740 could be cited in judgments without reference to statutes. $ ^{12} $ Second was the subsequent development of the sentencing guidelines found in “completed cases” (成案) and in the regulations of the Autumn Assizes. $ ^{13} $ Both events helped to modulate in a formal way the punishments that mad killers incurred depending on whom they had killed. crimes committed against senior family members. Once some changes had taken place, officials knew that the Ministry would likely approve of more proposals along the same lines, and change therefore followed from change. Since this policy theme did not automatically determine legislation, I explain each increase in punishment individually. As I observed above, this general increase in severity did not stem from a change in the legal conception of mad acts or mad people. For an act to qualify as “mad,” it still had to have been devoid of awareness, and the officials of the Ministry of Punishments still pressed field officials for clarifications when they detected traces of intentions in the actions of a potentially mad killer. The earliest example of a desire to adjust punishments for crimes committed “because of madness” was atypically systematic. In 1754, Shanxi provincial judge Jiang Zhou 蔣洲 (d. 1760) memorialized to “balance the sub-statute on killing because of madness” (請平瘋病殺人之例). $ ^{15} $ He first noted that those who had killed an older brother in a fit of madness had been sentenced in two conflicting ways: 1) to 3 years of penal servitude and 100 strokes of the heavy stick for the “Accidental killing of a superior or senior relative of the second degree” (過失殺期親尊長), $ ^{16} $ or 2) to “immediate decapitation” (斬立決) according to the statute on “killing one’s elder brother ...

secondary circumstance within the homicide of a close senior relative. These sentences were bolstered by two equally valid legal logics, namely the importance of intentions and the weight of seniority in intra-family crimes. Finding these judgments “particularly inconsistent” (殊未劃一), Jiang proposed to calibrate punishment according to the degree of family relation between killer and victim. $ ^{19} $ The killing of distant relatives would be redeemed monetarily according to the statute on killing relatives by accident. $ ^{20} $ One who killed a third- or fourth-degree relative (大小功尊長) whose death usually mandated “immediate decapitation” should be punished by the lower “decapitation after the Assizes.” $ ^{21} $ Finally, the sentence of “immediate decapitation” for killing second-degree seniors should be preserved, but an inserted slip should declare the insanity of the killer. The final decision should be left to the emperor. The Ministry of Punishments rejected Jiang’s proposal, but provincial officials soon had to face again the issues that Jiang had tried to disentangle. In 1756, the governor of Zhejiang confirmed a sentence of “decapitation after the Assizes” for a mad man who had killed his older brother. $ ^{22} $ This was one degree of lower than the penalty of “immediate decapitation” meted out to those who killed an older brother in a regular fight. But the Ministry ...

impose its judgment, but referred the case to the emperor, who entrusted the Nine Chief Ministers (九卿) with the final decision. $ ^{23} $ The congregated Ministers agreed with the governor to postpone death until “after the Assizes.” This way of sentencing madness-induced homicides of second-degree seniors remained in force until the end of the Qing. What made Jiang Zhou, the Ministry of Punishments, and the Zhejiang governor reluctant to treat the killing of seniors as cases of “accidental killing” was probably the view that a killing “because of madness” had an extra component that other “accidental killings” did not possess: direct aggression. Madmen killed with violent gestures. This feature of mad killings may have been what justified the Qianlong emperor's decision in 1761 to make the crime of "killing one's parents because of madness" punishable by dismemberment (凌遲), the harshest form of death penalty allowed by the Code. $ ^{24} $ His pronouncement was part of an edict that instructed provincial officials to execute all parricides swiftly—for fear they would take their own lives or die of starvation in prison—and to report the execution afterwards by palace memorial. $ ^{25} $ The law had long stressed the importance of hierarchical family relations, but in the 1760s the emperor pushed the same logic even further. unmatched_anchors.source_text: Too much is missing from these people's local worlds to understand the difficulties they faced, the way people related to them daily, and the way their treatment differed from the way other poor people were treated. But some themes recur. The commonest grievance I found in mad people's statements was a disagreement with this informal but persistent identification as mad or idiotic. Some people emphasized that this diagnosis came from others. Deng Tingmei 鄧廷梅 (1736): "My son had poxes and died. At once I became so angry that my mind became confused. My ears also became deaf. They said I had gone mad." $ ^{3} $ (出痘死了 一時把心氣成昏迷 耳朵也氣韜了 他們說我瘋了) Ding Wenbin 丁文彬 (1753), whose trial I discuss in 15.4, claimed to have been designated the Son of Heaven by the descendant of Confucius on orders of Shangdi the Lord on High, a title Ding's relatives did not recognize: "[My older brother] is an unreasonable person; he often calls me a fool" (他是不明道理的人 常罵我癡子). $ ^{4} $ And elsewhere: "I am not a fool, damn it! At home everybody says I'm a fool." (並不是癡子 可恨 家裏人人說我是癡子) "Fools" (癡子) were not necessarily persons of low intelligence. Those called "fools"

were often people who harbored fantasies that their abilities or positions would never allow them to fulfill (as in the expression "the fool who speaks his dream" 痴人說夢). $ ^{5} $ This was the case of "Great King of Shanxi" Lin Zhigong 林志功 (1762): "everybody says I am a fool and nobody believes me" (人人說我是痴子都不信我). $ ^{6} $

The fact that protests did not change their neighbors' and relatives' opinions probably vindicated or intensified some people's sense of injustice. $ ^{7} $ Lin Shiyuan's 林時元 statement was vehement: "In the third month of last year [1762], I suffered from some dizziness. They all said I was crazy and they invited a Daoist master to treat me. I am really not crazy at all!" $ ^{8} $ (我舊年三月有些頭暈的病 祇他們都說我瘋癲 叫道士替我醫治 我實在並不瘋) These men were caught in a language game they resented but could not escape, because the words they uttered to dispute their diagnosis only worked to confirm it.

They all said I was crazy and they invited a Daoist master to treat me. I am really not crazy at all!" $ ^{8} $ (我舊年三月有些頭暈的病 祇他們都說我瘋癲 叫道士替我醫治 我實在並不瘋) These men were caught in a language game they resented but could not escape, because the words they uttered to dispute their diagnosis only worked to confirm it.

Yao Zeng 姚增 came to Beijing from Zhejiang in search of the emperor. He “claimed that he could know the heavenly periods and the earthly principles; his words were empty and boastful” (自稱能知天時地理 言語虛誇). $ ^{10} $ In remembrance of his father, Liu Yuhou 劉裕後 wrote a book he called Dajiang pangshu 大江滂書 [Book of the torrential great river?], in which he compared his father to the sages, to immortals, and to Buddhas. $ ^{11} $ Lin Shiyuan called himself “the prime-teacher worthy” (第一師賢). $ ^{12} $

Bai Shengqi 白升奇 was born in Xianning 咸寧 (modern Xi'an, Shaanxi) in 1724 and had acquired some literacy when he was young, but soon "abandoned books for commerce" (棄書貿易), said the official report. $ ^{13} $ Since his case is better documented, let me recount at some length what happened to him and what compelled him to seek the help of Qing officials to justify himself. On his way south to Sichuan to sell pottery (販賣瓷器) in 1751, his boat had capsized and he had been greatly frightened. While traveling back home on the Han river, he had lost his belongings and his luggage (將衣物行李遺失). This loss was either the first sign of his madness or the event that triggered it. When Bai returned, his father confined him at home because he thought his son was mad.

The officials in charge first found Yang Xuan, the person to whom the slips were originally destined, but Yang did not know anyone called Li Ⅶ. He only knew two people called Bai:¹⁴ the head of a “community compact” (郷約) and “Bai the madman” (Bai fēngzi 白瘋子), who Yang claimed knew how to write. Governor Zhong Yin 鍾音 sent deputies to arrest Bai and search his house, where they found eleven bamboo strips and five slips of paper with the same handwriting as on the slips that had been delivered to the treasurer’s office. Bai Shengqi was then taken to the Governor’s complex, where, as was customary in cases of possible sedition, he was interrogated by the three highest officials in the province—the governor, the provincial judge, and the provincial treasurer.¹⁵

page_contexts.ocr_excerpt: ### 14.1 Grievances and reproaches As we have seen, some individuals in Chinese villages were identified as madmen (瘋子) or fools (痴子). More than a simple act of naming, this identification was a diagnosis sustained by the community—close relatives, neighbors, classmates, etc., who probably thought they were simply stating the obvious. "The local community called [Yang Huaizhen] a madman and stopped interacting with him" (鄉黨呼為瘋子 不與往來). $ ^{2} $ Too much is missing from these people's local worlds to understand the difficulties they faced, the way people related to them daily, and the way their treatment differed from the way other poor people were treated. But some themes recur. The commonest grievance I found in mad people's statements was a disagreement with this informal but persistent identification as mad or idiotic. Some people emphasized that this diagnosis came from others. Deng Tingmei 鄧廷梅 (1736): "My son had poxes and died. At once I became so angry that my mind became confused. My ears also became deaf. They said I had gone mad." $ ^{3} $ (出痘死了 一時把心氣成昏迷 耳朵也氣韜了 他們說我瘋了) Ding Wenbin 丁文彬 (1753), whose trial I discuss in 15.4, claimed to have been designated the Son of Heaven by the descendant of Confucius on orders of Shangdi the Lord on High, a title Ding's relatives did not recognize: "[My older brother] is an unreasonable person; he often calls me a fool" (他是不明道理的人 常罵我 ...

were often people who harbored fantasies that their abilities or positions would never allow them to fulfill (as in the expression "the fool who speaks his dream" 痴人說夢). $ ^{5} $ This was the case of "Great King of Shanxi" Lin Zhigong 林志功 (1762): "everybody says I am a fool and nobody believes me" (人人說我是痴子都不信我). $ ^{6} $ The fact that protests did not change their neighbors' and relatives' opinions probably vindicated or intensified some people's sense of injustice. $ ^{7} $ Lin Shiyuan's 林時元 statement was vehement: "In the third month of last year [1762], I suffered from some dizziness. They all said I was crazy and they invited a Daoist master to treat me. I am really not crazy at all!" $ ^{8} $ (我舊年三月有些頭暈的病 祇他們都說我瘋癲 叫道士替我醫治 我實在並不瘋) These men were caught in a language game they resented but could not escape, because the words they uttered to dispute their diagnosis only worked to confirm it. People whom others considered mad often objected to the way their relatives beat them, ignored them, wronged them, locked them up, and (maybe above all else) called them what they insisted they were not: mad. Any rough treatment received under the pretext that they were insane probably intensified their feelings of injustice. Lin Zhigong wrote in a poem that "turning human relations upside-down, the younger brother beats his elder" (顛倒人倫弟打兄). He Cheng'ao's 何成敖 words (1817), recorded after ...

Many people who were identified as mad constructed a superlative persona that made them glorious and excellent. Either before they were socially diagnosed as crazy or as a reaction to this label, they spoke of their dreams and visions and often sought official or imperial recognition for their mission or their virtue. Here I use a few cases to illustrate what a “crazy” persona could be like. Yao Zeng 姚增 came to Beijing from Zhejiang in search of the emperor. He “claimed that he could know the heavenly periods and the earthly principles; his words were empty and boastful” (自稱能知天時地理 言語虛誇). $ ^{10} $ In remembrance of his father, Liu Yuhou 劉裕後 wrote a book he called Dajiang pangshu 大江滂書 [Book of the torrential great river?], in which he compared his father to the sages, to immortals, and to Buddhas. $ ^{11} $ Lin Shiyuan called himself “the prime-teacher worthy” (第一師賢). $ ^{12} $ Bai Shengqi 白升奇 was born in Xianning 咸寧 (modern Xi'an, Shaanxi) in 1724 and had acquired some literacy when he was young, but soon "abandoned books for commerce" (棄書貿易), said the official report. $ ^{13} $ Since his case is better documented, let me recount at some length what happened to him and what compelled him to seek the help of Qing officials to justify himself. On his way south to Sichuan to sell pottery (販賣瓷器) in 1751, his boat had capsized and he had been greatly frightened. While traveling back ...

The officials in charge first found Yang Xuan, the person to whom the slips were originally destined, but Yang did not know anyone called Li Ⅶ. He only knew two people called Bai:¹⁴ the head of a “community compact” (郷約) and “Bai the madman” (Bai fēngzi 白瘋子), who Yang claimed knew how to write. Governor Zhong Yin 鍾音 sent deputies to arrest Bai and search his house, where they found eleven bamboo strips and five slips of paper with the same handwriting as on the slips that had been delivered to the treasurer’s office. Bai Shengqi was then taken to the Governor’s complex, where, as was customary in cases of possible sedition, he was interrogated by the three highest officials in the province—the governor, the provincial judge, and the provincial treasurer.¹⁵ It turned out that Bai's motives for presenting the slips were far from those of a rebel. Asked what his intentions were (究屬何心), he answered as follows: I lost my clothes and things in the Han River and no one pursued [this matter]. I have also been locked up for many years, which has been ghastly and difficult to bear. I wrote the names of people I know on bamboo strips and paper slips, and filled out the official posts, hoping to make them look for the lost objects for me. Because I do not see many people from year to year, I had [the slips] sent to the provincial treasurer's yamen, so that he could help me search the thing ...

to emperorship or if the self-aggrandizing “fifth year of Liqi” only sounded comforting in distressing times. $ ^{16} $ Zhang Tingyong 張廷用 also managed to sound politically subversive even as he constructed his "delirium" from daily objects and local names of people and organizations. $ ^{17} $ Of course his words were also designed to sound politically meaningful. As a youth, "Zhang Tingyong had participated in a [semi-annual local] examination to become a licentiate but had not been admitted. $ ^{18} $ This turned into madness/idiocy: he would constantly chant the Buddha's name; [the illness was] intermittent" (張廷用曾考試式童未能進取 染成瘋癡隨口念佛 時發時止). In 1748, having shaved his hair and queue (自剃髮辦), he went to Wutai Mountain to become a monk, but the bonzes in charge (當處僧人) made him return home. In 1756, when Zhang's illness became more serious, his family declared him to the county office through the head of the local community compact (經鄉約報官). The county issued chains and locks and delivered Zhang to his relatives, who were instructed to lock him up and keep watch over him (發給鎖封 交伊親屬鎖錮看守). $ ^{19} $ In 1760, he twisted his locks open and managed to flee to the city (扭鎖脫身 奔入城市), where he was captured by the magistrate's men. He was sent back home with new locks (仍發鎖封看守). The county government had files on both events (各有案). In 1767, while everybody was working in the fields, Zhang brok ...

manuscript notes that he may have written over the years or along the road (the report did not tell). He was caught again. As the magistrate in charge of his case reported: The text is mediocre and pedestrian, preposterous and confused, constantly redundant and not forming full sentences. But among the words we find "White-rain dragon-king association," "Society of the Ten Buddhas," and the names of people in those associations, such as "Liu Guangrong," "Association Leader Cao," and "Association Leader Wang." I am very afraid that [these names] hide something else, such as [sutra?] reading associations. 閱其鄙俚誕妄迷係重複雷同 不成語句 惟字內載有白雨龍王會、十佛會及會中人劉光榮 曹會長 王會長名 甚恐有念□會別情 Symbolically loaded language made officials apprehensive. Zhang Tingyong's slips seemed to mention the kind of religious association that was often banned under the label of “devious cult” (邪教). Such religious groups were common in Qing China, but the state feared they might develop into large-scale organizations that could rival the state in their sway over “people’s hearts.” $ ^{20} $ The officials who interrogated the local leaders and the people Zhang Tingyong named in his notes soon found that there was no rebellion in the making. The “Society of the Ten Buddhas” was the name of a long-banned association that was named after ten days representing the birthdays of ten gods and Buddhas (神佛誕日 十項相傳). The report specified ...

who sought his services and chose auspicious days for marriages and funerals (代人酹神祈禱 并選擇婚葬日期), but practiced no occult arts. The lofty-sounding “dragon-king association” turned out to be an agricultural cult and “white rain” the name local peasants gave to hailstones (冰雹). From the fourth to the eighth lunar month, the villagers went to the neighboring temple of the dragon spirit.... to burn incense and paper to [?] and pray (村農至附近龍神廟內□帶香褚靼流祈禱) that the hail would not harm the crops: “there are in fact no such things as gatherings of crowds to read sutras or the establishment of devious cults” (實無聚眾念經、創立邪教情事). The practice of presenting incense at the local dragon-god temple was in fact consistent with what the Code called “charitable societies that conduct prayers in the spring and fall” (在本村神龍廟靼月進香 與律載春秋義社以行祈报者相同 似無庸置議). $ ^{21} $ The Governor of Shanxi clarified small details: under interrogation in Zhili soon after he was caught, Zhang Tingyong had mentioned the existence of “one effortless knife kept by xianzi [i.e., a sage]” (顺刀一把是賢子收著). The “knife kept by the sage” was not the fantastic weapon or religious object that officials feared it might be. The Shanxi Governor, who had a copy of Zhang’s statement, discovered that Zhang’s house was near a mountain and that the family owned an old knife for protection against wild animals, which was kept in the house of “this crimin ...

Because Zhang had not impinged on the sanctity of imperial symbols, he was not physically punished. As the 1767 report concluded, “it has been established that this criminal Zhang Tingyong is indeed mad; according to the [1762] sub-statute [on those who have committed a killing because of madness], the Yingzhou magistrate shall be ordered to confine him and take the proper precautions, so that [Zhang] will not be allowed to break out and stir up trouble again” (该犯张廷用實屬瘋癲 應飭令應州知州照例嚴加鎖禁防範 毋使再以出境滋事). We know of Bai Shengqi and Zhang Tingyong through third-person reports on their words and acts. We now turn to someone who left his own writings, and whose view on life we therefore know much more about than Bai’s and Zhang’s. ### 14.3 “Idle steed” Lin Zhigong and his writings Lin Zhigong's 林志功 case is one of the most interesting to have survived in the records. $ ^{22} $ Lin was born in 1708 in Changshan 常山 county in Quzhou 衢州, the westernmost prefecture of Zhejiang, near the border of Jiangxi province. He became mad with grief after his wife and one of his two sons died of disease early in 1735. His relatives tried to have him exorcized, but without success. A new marriage was nonetheless arranged between Lin and a young bride from Yushan 玉山, a county west of Changshan in Jiangxi.       "page_no": 529,
      "ocr_excerpt": "### 13.1 Discussions of madness in legal reports Judicial depositions and their biases Much of what we know about the kinds of behavior Qing people called “mad” comes from “routine memorials of the Office of Scrutiny for the Ministry of Punishments” (刑科题本). $ ^{1} $ These reports were written by the highest officials in the provincial bureaucracy (governors and governors-general) as the final step in the sentencing of a crime, or by the Ministry of Punishments in response to these reports. $ ^{2} $ Each memorial gave a detailed account of an entire case and of the way the judicial system had processed it. Most cited the “depositions” (gong 供) of the people involved: the “criminal” (as a person under trial was usually called), his or her relatives and neighbors, local leaders, and anyone the magistrate found relevant to interrogate. $ ^{3} $ These statements illustrate how common people spoke of madness and dealt with it, but they should be used with great circumspection. Depositions were not neutrally collected “representations of madness.” As we have seen, Qing law accepted as “mad” only acts that were entirely devoid of awareness.
