unmatched_anchors.source_text: Much of what we know about the kinds of behavior Qing people called “mad” comes from “routine memorials of the Office of Scrutiny for the Ministry of Punishments” (刑科题本). $ ^{1} $ These reports were written by the highest officials in the provincial bureaucracy (governors and governors-general) as the final step in the sentencing of a crime, or by the Ministry of Punishments in response to these reports. $ ^{2} $ Each memorial gave a detailed account of an entire case and of the way the judicial system had processed it. Most cited the “depositions” (gong 供) of the people involved: the “criminal” (as a person under trial was usually called), his or her relatives and neighbors, local leaders, and anyone the magistrate found relevant to interrogate. $ ^{3} $ These statements illustrate how common people spoke of madness and dealt with it, but they should be used with great circumspection.

These are external limits to what “routine memorials” (tiben) will tell us. Even more important, the content of the depositions recorded in the reports was also pre-oriented. Statements found their way into tiben by a complex process. During an official interrogation, the “criminal” and the witnesses gave “oral depositions” (□供), which a clerk recorded “in a mixture of spoken and written style corresponding to what was actually said.” $ ^{4} $ If witnesses disagreed or if there were discrepancies between their depositions and the material evidence, the magistrate would press everyone to tell the true story. Once all doubts and contradictions were cleared, he “ordered a clerk to write down for the criminal a formal confession of his offense, including key-points in the story and composed in a particular style.” For a condemnation to be acceptable, the criminal had to endorse this version of events.

Yasuhiko Karasawa (2007) has noticed broad discrepancies between draft depositions and the way they were cited in later reports. $ ^{5} $ He has shown that during the course of the Qing the legal secretaries who composed the final drafts of tiben increasingly tended to edit testimonies so as to confirm the magistrate’s (and the governor’s) findings, removing from the depositions lies, smokescreens, gaps or contradictions in evidence, and all the possibly truthful details that did not appear in the official narrative of the case (which in turn purported to be based on the confessions).

the reports. Interestingly, all made their way into depositions. $ ^{8} $ Bureaucratic jargon is particularly important because it appeared everywhere. Only good knowledge of legal texts and the reading of numerous tiben allow to separate the formulaic from words that the witnesses would have been more likely to recognize as their own. Before I turn to the depositions themselves, let me survey the main set phrases that punctuated legal reports and how we can use them to some profit despite their seemingly opaque uniformity.

This being said, the language of reports suggests that madness could be discerned in one’s facial expression or countenance (神色). The visage of many mad men was described as “confused” (神色昏迷). $ ^{11} $ By contrast, the aspect and language of a recovered person were said to be “sober” or “clear” (清爽). $ ^{12} $ A longer set phrase specified that the recovered killer’s “facial expression was sober and his speech clear” (神清色爽 語言明晰). $ ^{13} $ Since officials knew that legal madness was all or nothing: the reports they wrote therefore made as strong a distinction as possible between the normal and the mad: a dumb-struck expression contrasted with a sober one; incoherent speech with clear language.

Tiben often cast the events that had led to a killing into the predetermined script of how the law expected madness cases to unfold. Because most mad killings were committed during sudden fits and because the law defined madness as utter unawareness of one's own actions, the vast majority of memorials specified that on such-and-such a date, the would-be killer "got madness illness, with intermittent fits; when it flares up, he is unaware of people and things" (得了瘋病 時發時止 發時不醒人事). $ ^{14} $ This was how the prototypical scenario of a mad homicide started.

Medical case files also contained formulaic turns of phrase, but they tended to be more flexible, perhaps because they recorded symptoms that physicians had observed (by contrast with both writers of medical treatises, who were trying to present general arguments, and legal officials or secretaries, who had not witnessed the acts of the people they were describing) and because these symptoms could be important to the establishment of a therapy. $ ^{16} $ The readers of medical case files also differed from those of legal reports. Whereas legal reports were meant to be assessed by superiors or equals, medical records were written for the relatives of patients or for readers of collections of case files. Although physicians occasionally used formulaic phrases (often descriptions of symptoms drawn from the Yellow Emperor's Inner Canon) in their case files, they had nothing to gain from channeling all their cases into the same convenient mold when the uniqueness of each treatment was precisely what brought them distinction.

rebind_candidates.anchor_excerpt: Even when depositions recorded people’s voices accurately, however, they did not necessarily reflect what commoners would have said about the people under trial if they had been interacting with them or talking about them in a daily context. Since we have no access to the words that the witnesses actually pronounced, the best we can do is to assess each memorial on an individual basis while keeping in mind all the caveats I have just outlined. $ ^{7} $

This being said, the language of reports suggests that madness could be discerned in one’s facial expression or countenance (神色). The visage of many mad men was described as “confused” (神色昏迷). $ ^{11} $ By contrast, the aspect and language of a recovered person were said to be “sober” or “clear” (清爽). $ ^{12} $ A longer set phrase specified that the recovered killer’s “facial expression was sober and his speech clear” (神清色爽 語言明晰). $ ^{13} $ Since officials knew that legal madness was all or nothing: the reports they wrote therefore made as strong a distinction as possible between the normal and the mad: a dumb-struck expression contrasted with a sober one; incoherent speech with clear language.

In contrast with medical texts, which usually focused on organic processes, most people cited in legal reports attributed madness to an extreme emotional experience. Some became crazy from fright, Men Fa 門發 (1816) because he had almost drowned in his buffaloes' water pit, Bai Shengqi 白升奇 (1751) because his boat had capsized. $ ^{50} $ A physician was said to have gone mad because other doctors had mocked his failures. $ ^{51} $ Many reported falling ill because they had lost a young child. Bannerman Jixiang (1816) became crazy in 1815 soon after his son was killed by the collapse of a wall in the Beijing house where they lived: "I often missed my son, then I got mucus-confusion disorder" (我時常想念兒子

Disorders that caused discomfort in the chest (including cardio-vascular disorders) were easily experienced as the doing of mucus. The Jiaqing Emperor (r. 1796-1820) died on the evening of September 2, 1820 at the age of fifty-nine, one day after he had felt “discomforted” (不豫) upon arriving in the imperial summer residence of Rehe. $ ^{86} $ A

criminal became poorer and more troubled day by day. His stasis ["depression"] turned into an illness and he contracted 'confusion by mucus.' In the seventh month of Qianlong 47 [1782], he jumped from the city wall and hurt his leg falling. $ ^{89} $ (該犯日益貧難 抑鬱成病得有痰迷之症 曾於四十七年七月間 跳墮城牆 將腿跌傷) Finally, a terse entry in a body of administrative regulations explained that since the conduct of an unnamed juren 舉人 was caused by "mucus confusion," he should not be deprived of his degree. $ ^{90} $

page_contexts.ocr_excerpt: ### 13.1 Discussions of madness in legal reports Judicial depositions and their biases Much of what we know about the kinds of behavior Qing people called “mad” comes from “routine memorials of the Office of Scrutiny for the Ministry of Punishments” (刑科题本). $ ^{1} $ These reports were written by the highest officials in the provincial bureaucracy (governors and governors-general) as the final step in the sentencing of a crime, or by the Ministry of Punishments in response to these reports. $ ^{2} $ Each memorial gave a detailed account of an entire case and of the way the judicial system had processed it. Most cited the “depositions” (gong 供) of the people involved: the “criminal” (as a person under trial was usually called), his or her relatives and neighbors, local leaders, and anyone the magistrate found relevant to interrogate. $ ^{3} $ These statements illustrate how common people spoke of madness and dealt with it, but they should be used with great circumspection. Depositions were not neutrally collected “representations of madness.” As we have seen, Qing law accepted as “mad” only acts that were entirely devoid of awareness. This extreme definition and the law’s narrow interest in killings explain why depositions focused disproportionately on fits of furor. Because the content of a mad killer’s words was usually

irrelevant to a homicide investigation, legal reports usually said little about what may have constituted mad language, but this vagueness about mad speech does not mean that the people under trial were always unable to speak coherently, or that only the utterly delirious were identified as mad in village communities. Legal documents also tell us little about the non-violent gestures and words that may have characterized local madmen, and about the daily interactions between them and their relatives, even when the depositions purported to be precisely about that. Certain derangements that people may have called “mad” (unmotivated sorrow, love-sickness, flights of fancy, etc.) were rarely recorded in legal sources because they were not seen as having led to the killings, or when they were, the cases in question were not sentenced as if they had been committed “because of madness.” Finally, these documents give no access to the “discourse processes through which social and emotional realities are constituted in ordinary talk and interaction” (White 1992: 38). These are external limits to what “routine memorials” (tiben) will tell us. Even more important, the content of the depositions recorded in the reports was also pre-oriented. Statements found their way into tiben by a complex process. During an official interrogation, the “criminal” and the witnesses gave “oral depositions” ...

The magistrate composed a summary of the entire affair and proposed a temporary sentence according to the relevant laws. He sent his report to his immediate superior the prefect, along with the formal depositions (招狀) of the criminal and of all the witnesses, reconstituted from the clerk's notes. I have already discussed the several reviews this preliminary sentencing had to go through before it could be confirmed (see 10). The depositions we read in these provincial reports were thus several times removed from the words that the parties had originally uttered. Yasuhiko Karasawa (2007) has noticed broad discrepancies between draft depositions and the way they were cited in later reports. $ ^{5} $ He has shown that during the course of the Qing the legal secretaries who composed the final drafts of tiben increasingly tended to edit testimonies so as to confirm the magistrate’s (and the governor’s) findings, removing from the depositions lies, smokescreens, gaps or contradictions in evidence, and all the possibly truthful details that did not appear in the official narrative of the case (which in turn purported to be based on the confessions). In some madness cases, all the witnesses were cited as having used the same words in the same order to describe what happened on the day of a crime. Even the killer used the same words despite claiming that he had no memory of what had happ ...

Depositions were edited translations of orality rather than direct transcriptions. Conventions of legal reporting such as the use of the written style and the selective editing of depositions make it difficult to assess what terms common people used to describe madness. Very specific depositions and those that “sound oral” were probably closer to the original words of the witnesses than the conventionally formulated ones. The memorials that quoted the witnesses as having said different things with different words in two different trial sessions are also more likely to have been authentic. The fact that these depositions consistently sounded more oral seems to confirm this view. It remains possible, however, that in many cases some seemingly oral usage reflected a secretary’s use of a vernacular style of writing as a device to enhance the authenticity of the depositions. Even when depositions recorded people’s voices accurately, however, they did not necessarily reflect what commoners would have said about the people under trial if they had been interacting with them or talking about them in a daily context. Since we have no access to the words that the witnesses actually pronounced, the best we can do is to assess each memorial on an individual basis while keeping in mind all the caveats I have just outlined. $ ^{7} $ ## The interest of the formulaic The expressions concerning ...

the reports. Interestingly, all made their way into depositions. $ ^{8} $ Bureaucratic jargon is particularly important because it appeared everywhere. Only good knowledge of legal texts and the reading of numerous tiben allow to separate the formulaic from words that the witnesses would have been more likely to recognize as their own. Before I turn to the depositions themselves, let me survey the main set phrases that punctuated legal reports and how we can use them to some profit despite their seemingly opaque uniformity. The concepts we see in documents were more than “background noise” that should be reduced before we can get to the undistorted facts. These objects were precisely those that institutions and practitioners purported to discuss and act upon. Formulaic phrases may have given little idea of what the person they described looked and sounded like, but they spoke eloquently of what the legal field expected madness to sound and look like. Judicial reports almost invariably noted that the eyes of killers who had just been arrested “were staring straight” (兩目直瞪 or 兩目直視) and that their “words were incoherent” (語無論次). These words described the stereotypically stupefied-and-rambling madman who could not provide the deposition that was necessary for sentencing to take place. Since these reports were written only after painstaking investigations had confirmed that the pers ...

recognized by these outward signs would be putting the result (an ex post facto report phrased in unequivocal terms) before the process of identification. This being said, the language of reports suggests that madness could be discerned in one’s facial expression or countenance (神色). The visage of many mad men was described as “confused” (神色昏迷). $ ^{11} $ By contrast, the aspect and language of a recovered person were said to be “sober” or “clear” (清爽). $ ^{12} $ A longer set phrase specified that the recovered killer’s “facial expression was sober and his speech clear” (神清色爽 語言明晰). $ ^{13} $ Since officials knew that legal madness was all or nothing: the reports they wrote therefore made as strong a distinction as possible between the normal and the mad: a dumb-struck expression contrasted with a sober one; incoherent speech with clear language. Tiben often cast the events that had led to a killing into the predetermined script of how the law expected madness cases to unfold. Because most mad killings were committed during sudden fits and because the law defined madness as utter unawareness of one's own actions, the vast majority of memorials specified that on such-and-such a date, the would-be killer "got madness illness, with intermittent fits; when it flares up, he is unaware of people and things" (得了瘋病 時發時止 發時不醒人事). $ ^{14} $ This was how the prototypical scenario of a mad ...

The confession was not an admission of guilt doubled with repentance (though depositions sometimes contained statements of regret, dismay, or contrition), but the killer's formulation of the crime for which he would be sentenced—his act as it was characterized by the law. Since by definition he did not remember his acts, his confession could only be the ratification of the version of the crime that officials had settled upon after investigation. Medical case files also contained formulaic turns of phrase, but they tended to be more flexible, perhaps because they recorded symptoms that physicians had observed (by contrast with both writers of medical treatises, who were trying to present general arguments, and legal officials or secretaries, who had not witnessed the acts of the people they were describing) and because these symptoms could be important to the establishment of a therapy. $ ^{16} $ The readers of medical case files also differed from those of legal reports. Whereas legal reports were meant to be assessed by superiors or equals, medical records were written for the relatives of patients or for readers of collections of case files. Although physicians occasionally used formulaic phrases (often descriptions of symptoms drawn from the Yellow Emperor's Inner Canon) in their case files, they had nothing to gain from channeling all their cases into the same convenient mo ...

affliction by nine different terms. $ ^{17} $ Putting aside those related to “mucus” for the moment, they were “madness illness” (the official term for madness), fengbing 風病 (lit. “Wind illness,” but homophonous with the term that meant “madness illness”), fengdian 風顛 (“madness”; lit. Wind dian), $ ^{18} $ fengkuang 風狂 (“madness”; lit. Wind kuang), diankuang 顛狂 (“insanity”), $ ^{19} $ fengkuang bing 風狂病 (“madness illness”), fengkuang bingzheng 風狂病症 (“madness disorder”), and fengdian bingzheng 風顛病症 (“madness disorder”). $ ^{20} $ We cannot assume that people used the term “madness illness” orally. Considering that fengdian and fengkuang were intelligible on their own and were widely used in non-judicial contexts, we should probably interpret the compounds fengkuang bing, fengkuang bingzheng, and fengdian bingzheng—which were clearly not oral—as bureaucratic jargon. The suffixes bing and bingzheng may have been used to make clear that the law considered madness to be an illness. $ ^{21} $ Apart from the pair-turned-compound diankuang, all the above terms contain the sound feng. A clerk or official who wanted to transcribe the sound feng could choose between the homophonous 瘋 and 風. The fact that the report on Deng Tingmei’s case used 瘋 only when quoting the letter of the law suggests that the 瘋 usage was still marginal at the time. $ ^{22} $ The last two “terms for madness” emplo ...

A flurry of articles on that subject appeared in the first year of publication of several academic or semi-academic journals in 1979 and 1980, soon after the Third Plenum of the Eleventh Central Committee of the Chinese Communist Party in late 1978 had confirmed the fall of the Gang of Four and modified conditions of scholarship. $ ^{5} $

An even more recent wave of scholarship on wenziyu has by and large retained the view that the Qing indiscriminately repressed young Chinese thinkers and intellectuals. $ ^{6} $ Anecdotal accounts and emphasis on the unfairness of the condemnations are still the chief aspects of this scholarship. Now that the anti-Manchu sentiment has disappeared (since the Manchus are now retrospectively presented as a “minority nationality” within the broad Chinese nation), the fault for wenziyu is rejected on “feudal government” in general.

As many of the men I discuss in this chapter, Chai Shilu's brother Shijin 世進 came to official attention while trying to present a text to an imperial administrator. Donations of letters and texts were not always illegal, but the Qing code contained a law that forbade the presentation of texts in order to gain recognition or employment: seeking advancement under a false pretense was to be punished with 100 strokes of the heavy stick. $ ^{9} $

Despite the heavy punishment incurred, all sorts of people presented texts to officials to attract their attention. Many of the cases I discuss in this chapter commenced in a similar fashion, when “mad people” contacted the officialdom to voice grievances, to express praise for the emperor, or to seek advice or employment. $ ^{10} $ Education commissioners’ (學證) constant travels, their proximity to licentiates, and their moral reputation made them favorite targets for grievance bearers and letter writers. $ ^{11} $

Despite the heavy punishment incurred, all sorts of people presented texts to officials to attract their attention. Many of the cases I discuss in this chapter commenced in a similar fashion, when “mad people” contacted the officialdom to voice grievances, to express praise for the emperor, or to seek advice or employment. $ ^{10} $ Education commissioners’ (學證) constant travels, their proximity to licentiates, and their moral reputation made them favorite targets for grievance bearers and letter writers. $ ^{11} $

Offerings of dubious texts were matters serious enough to be discussed in palace memorials, a form of document designed for reporting on important state matters. Governors and Governors-general usually conducted interrogations in person and were responsible for mobilizing lower officials for swift and thorough investigations. Officials scrutinized the family background and network of relations of the “criminals,” their possible involvement in unlawful activities, and whether they were concealing more “illicit writings.” $ ^{12} $

Official suspicion about these texts contrasted with the hopes of those who presented them. Wang Zhaoji 王肇基 presented a poem in praise of the emperor to a Shanxi prefect in 1751, but his eulogy was found to include "recklessly seditious passages" (狂妄悖逆之處) and words that "slandered the sages" (毀謗聖賢). $ ^{13} $ As he confessed, "In offering a poem respectfully wishing ten thousand years to the Empress Dowager, I was only doing my utmost as a small commoner. All I wanted was to please His Imperial

Majesty”(我獻詩恭祝皇太后萬歲 不過盡我小民之心 欲求皇上喜歡的意思). He was sentenced to death by beating, a heavy punishment considering that officials admitted he did it “without awareness”(無知).¹⁴ The use of this irregular punishment indicates that Wang’s “crime” was considered serious enough to justify disregard for penal denominations, but that it was not completely on a par with treason or sedition, which would have led to the execution of all of Wang’s male relatives and the enslavement of his female kin.

What type of political regime could feel threatened by such words? In what contexts were expressions of loyalty unacceptable? Are we being misled by what we feel is a clear boundary between madness and crime? I seek answers to these questions in four interrelated domains ...

Belief in the power of writing can be observed both in their naive hope that a text would bring rewards when presented to imperial administrators, and in the often frenzied reaction of officials to these text-bearers. truly lost their heart and become sickly mad also turn to writing and [pretend to suffer from] madness illness, hoping to bend the law. $ ^{19} $ This [matter] has to do with local mores and people's hearts. $ ^{20} $ The original memorial has been copied and sent to every Governor and Governor-general for their perusal, so that they can more easily report [on the current situation in their province?]. They have been ordered to instruct their subordinates immediately that when they encounter such people suffering from madness illness, they should take strict preventive measures of guard and precaution, and that no slackness and idleness will be allowed. 陳宏謀請鎖錮瘋人一摺 所奏亦有所見 向來此等瘋人病發 原定有嚴加約束之例 但行之日久 地方官不能實力奉行 以致旋鎖旋釋 甚至任其播弄筆墨 滋生事端 而匪人中實系喪心病狂者亦轉藉詞瘋病 冀為骫法 與風俗人心甚有關係 著於各督撫奏事之便 將原摺抄寄閱看 令其即飭所屬遇有此等瘋病之人 應預為嚴行看守防範 毋得稍有弛懈 Chen knew that the mix of madness with a brush had already led to sedition cases, but the measures he proposed were not very concrete. Indeed, as far as I know, his proposal was never implemented. His claim that some criminals pretended to be mad in order to escape punishment made little sense, since, as we shall see, men who were recognized as mad were commonly executed when they wrote seditious texts. ## Lecturing the emperor: two cases Inappropriate statements did not have to be treasonous in order to infuriate the sovereign. Chinese)—was so grieved by his conduct that she had committed suicide. $ ^{21} $ Ula Nara was born in 1718 and had become Empress in 1750. She had indeed accompanied the emperor on his fourth Southern Tour in early 1765, but for reasons that are still unclear, the monarch suddenly had ordered her to return to Beijing. She either died of grief or committed suicide in the palace the following year, after she had obviously fallen out of favor. $ ^{22} $ Yan presented his message to a Shanxi official in 1777. Written in the form of a palace memorial, it was a sympathetic reproof of the sovereign's alleged misconduct: Empress Na[la] was plain and excellent, principled and chaste, and had been blessed with much doting. Seeing that His Majesty, despite being over five-decades old and busy with numerous and complicated matters of state, still showed special favor to ladies as in the past, she was afraid this was not a good way of nurturing the Sagely imperial body. For this reason, and because she had to endure such an offense, she made light of her own life. 納皇后質美節烈 多蒙寵愛 見皇上年過五旬 國事紛繁 若仍如前寵幸 恐非善養聖體 是以故加挺觸輕生 Yan was sentenced to death by dismemberment, probably as much for his unbecoming address as for having forged a palace memorial. The case of Feng Qiyan 馮起炎 took place in Shanxi in 1783, when Feng was 31 sui. $ ^{23} $ He had been born a Zhao 趙, but when his father had died his mothe ...

In 1781, however, he moved to a school attached to the military garrison of Pingyang, where he made a living copying documents (借住平陽營子識文 在茲家學堂抄寫文冊餬口). There, he kept a crumbling copy of the Four Books, an Old-Text version of the Classic of Poetry, and a copy of the well-liked Romance of the Western Chamber (Xixiang ji 西廂記). Despite having found employment, Feng grew so impoverished that he became depressed and restless (贫苦難度 憂鬱無聊) and his despondency eventually turned into an illness (抑鬱成病). $ ^{24} $ In 1782, after he caught what later reports called “mucus-confusion disorder” (痰迷之症), he hurt his legs trying to commit suicide by jumping from the Pingyang city wall. A few months later, just after the lunar New Year, he left for Beijing, planning to rejoin the Zhao line (意欲歸宗) and to study trade (學習買賣). He arrived 15 days later, carrying commentaries he had written on the Classic of Poetry and the Book of Changes. Since Feng had been a student for his entire life, his Beijing relatives urged him to abandon his commercial plans. His uncle gave him 1000 in copper cash and his younger brother 500 in Beijing currency (大錢). In an inn on the way back home, Feng Qiyan heard that the imperial carriage was coming hither to vi ...

Some of the doubts that officials expressed about the madness (or “besottedness”) of text bearers probably resulted from bureaucratic necessity. When reporting on a case, they could not assume that one who looked or sounded crazy was crazy. This “institutionalized strategy” (Bourdieu 1990: 138) on the part of officials was the object of no specific rule, but it was strongly encouraged in bureaucratic practice. Those officials who embodied this fear of mad-sounding speech were praised, whereas officials who simply assumed that the authors of these texts were mad were punished if their negligence was later discovered. $ ^{25} $ But even when officials had found no illegal activities behind a suspicious text, they were not necessarily clement. Although Feng Qiyan was eventually recognized to have suffered from “mucus confusion,” his punishment was not lenient. Feng Qiyan’s having illicitly written a petition for a trifling marriage matter and intended to present it in front of the imperial convoy was truly stupid, reckless, and illegal. Though his planning to marry someone’s daughters resembles besotted confusion, it remains that his words are clear. And how can we know that he is not cunningly pretending to be confused in order to unleash his mad barking? It would thus be unsuitable to be even slightly indulgent. request_prompt.system: 你是 FNM 注释修补助手（同时处理 endnote 和 footnote 两种 note_system）。只处理已经确认的 unresolved cluster，不要改 section、note zone、标题或原文。优先依据页面截图判断，不要被 OCR 坏掉的数字误导。如果截图已经足够清楚，就不要退回 needs_review。你只能输出 JSON 数组；每项 action 只能是 match、ignore_ref、synthesize_anchor、synthesize_note_item 或 needs_review。match 需要 note_item_id、anchor_id、confidence、reason；ignore_ref 需要 anchor_id、confidence、reason；synthesize_anchor 仅在正文里能找到一个独一无二的短语锚点、但该锚点没有对应的结构化 anchor 记录时使用，需要 note_item_id、anchor_phrase（从正文中原样抄写的 3~12 词唯一短语，不要自编）、confidence、reason；synthesize_note_item 仅在截图里能清楚看到同页注释文本、但 OCR / 结构化流程完全没产出 note item 时使用，需要 anchor_id、marker、note_text、confidence、reason；若某条 note 当前只是错误地绑到了 synthetic / 跨页锚点，而截图清楚显示它应改绑到当前显式锚点，也直接用 match，不要 needs_review。needs_review 需要 reason。

cluster.chapter_title: Mad acts, mad speech, and mad people in Chinese medicine and law

unmatched_anchors.source_text: Lin Shiyuan was captured after he threw a roof tile to the governor of Fujian. Two slips of red paper attached to the tile were covered with “crazy and nonsensical words that are difficult to understand” (狂謬難解之詞). When the three highest officials in the province interrogated Lin to assess whether these were treasonous words in disguise, he spoke of dragon mothers and dream revelations, and claimed to be “the prime teacher-worthy” (第一師賢). The saying that Lin cited to prove he was not insane may have swayed listeners in other circumstances, but Lin’s relatives and neighbors insisted he was mad. $ ^{2} $ When asked what illness he suffered from, he replied: “In the third month of last year, I had a bit of a dizziness illness; they all said I was crazy and they invited a Taoist master to treat

Ng’s reasoning rests on two Qing developments: 1) in the 1730s, the government ordered families to confine their mad relatives even when they had committed no crime; 2) in the eighteenth century, legislators meted out increasingly severe sentences upon those who killed someone in a fit of madness. Ng argued that these measures, which she attributed to a more-assumed-than-argued Manchu obsession with social control, represented a sea change in Chinese attitudes toward madness. But the “from illness to deviance” thesis is largely an artifact of the way Ng staged her documents. $ ^{4} $ When Ng claims that legal views replaced medical ones, it is in fact her analytical gaze that leaps from medical to legal documents, never to look back. As I shall show in chapters 11 to 15, medical, legal, religious, and popular views often overlapped, and they kept interacting in concrete social contexts throughout Qing times. Even Qing law consistently treated insanity as an illness.

I would like to study shifting conceptions and practices without presuming that they were hemmed around any kind of “madness in itself,” however ungrasable. But without an ontological coagulant, what are we studying when we claim to be writing the history of “madness”?¹⁶ Harnessing Foucault’s (1976) critique of the notion of “sexuality,” we could say that “madness” brings together in an artificial unity a disparate array of notions, people, ways of speaking, and forms of behavior.¹⁷ I also find these words by Foucault suggestive: “I have personally never written that madness does not exist, but this could be written, because, for phenomenology, madness exists but it is not a thing, whereas we should say on the contrary that madness does not exist, but that it is not nothing for that reason (mais qu’elle n’est pas rien pour autant).”¹⁸ Words have referents even if these are not natural objects. One of my aims will be to highlight this artificial unity of “madness” and to refashion the resulting dispersion

I would like to study shifting conceptions and practices without presuming that they were hemmed around any kind of “madness in itself,” however ungrasable. But without an ontological coagulant, what are we studying when we claim to be writing the history of “madness”?¹⁶ Harnessing Foucault’s (1976) critique of the notion of “sexuality,” we could say that “madness” brings together in an artificial unity a disparate array of notions, people, ways of speaking, and forms of behavior.¹⁷ I also find these words by Foucault suggestive: “I have personally never written that madness does not exist, but this could be written, because, for phenomenology, madness exists but it is not a thing, whereas we should say on the contrary that madness does not exist, but that it is not nothing for that reason (mais qu’elle n’est pas rien pour autant).”¹⁸ Words have referents even if these are not natural objects. One of my aims will be to highlight this artificial unity of “madness” and to refashion the resulting dispersion

Nouns are the Trojan horses of ontology. “A substantive misleads one into looking for a substance. One populates the world with ethereal beings, the shadowy companions of the substantive. One could legitimately call the science of these fictitious entities metaphysics.” $ ^{19} $ Speaking of “representations of madness,” for instance, implies that people observed something called “madness” and then created representations of “it.” But there is no need to identify a “shadowy companion”—a substance—behind the substantive “madness” before we go further. What counts as madness in any culture is defined socially, expressed through cultural idioms, and handled in culturally meaningful fashion. No timeless and pre-cultural “madness” (were we even able to pinpoint such a thing) would suffice to make these representations and practices intelligible. $ ^{20} $

One could legitimately call the science of these fictitious entities metaphysics.” $ ^{19} $ Speaking of “representations of madness,” for instance, implies that people observed something called “madness” and then created representations of “it.” But there is no need to identify a “shadowy companion”—a substance—behind the substantive “madness” before we go further. What counts as madness in any culture is defined socially, expressed through cultural idioms, and handled in culturally meaningful fashion. No timeless and pre-cultural “madness” (were we even able to pinpoint such a thing) would suffice to make these representations and practices intelligible. $ ^{20} $

What makes comparisons possible is that Chinese people—experts and commoners alike—identified as mad the same disorders of behavior, speech, affect, and perception that we usually include in an intuitive category of “madness.” $ ^{21} $ The legal and medical practices I study often relied on this self-evidence, on people’s intuition that some acts naturally deserved to be called kuang, dian, or feng, three words that usually meant “mad” or “acting madly” in the same vague sense as the English words I just translated them by.

To avoid making generalizations that will predictably not apply to all people of all periods, I use the notion of “prototype.” $ ^{22} $ Most definitions are prototypical in that they mark out the most typical members of a given category (“tables,” “burrowing animals”). Categories contain more than prototypes, but their members gradually radiate away from the prototypical hub. A three-legged chair is thus a less prototypical chair than a four-legged one, the platypus a less prototypical mammal than the elephant.

page_contexts.ocr_excerpt: # Mad acts, mad words, and mad people in Chinese medicine and law I have a two-phrase saying: “At first you’re crazy. Past the immortals’ peak, you’re a divine immortal.” $ ^{1} $ 我有兩句俗話 初頭是瘋癲 過了仙嶺是神仙 Lin Shiyuan 林時元, 1763 Lin Shiyuan was captured after he threw a roof tile to the governor of Fujian. Two slips of red paper attached to the tile were covered with “crazy and nonsensical words that are difficult to understand” (狂謬難解之詞). When the three highest officials in the province interrogated Lin to assess whether these were treasonous words in disguise, he spoke of dragon mothers and dream revelations, and claimed to be “the prime teacher-worthy” (第一師賢). The saying that Lin cited to prove he was not insane may have swayed listeners in other circumstances, but Lin’s relatives and neighbors insisted he was mad. $ ^{2} $ When asked what illness he suffered from, he replied: “In the third month of last year, I had a bit of a dizziness illness; they all said I was crazy and they invited a Taoist master to treat

### 1.1 Historiography: insights and oversights Madness has just begun to attract the attention of historians of China. Vivien Ng's Madness in Late Imperial China (1990), until recently the only book on this topic, examined the way mad people were handled in Qing society and how their treatment changed over time. Ng was the first scholar to dissect a number of legal cases in which madness played a role, but the book's main thesis—that madness was transformed "from an illness to a form of criminal deviance" in the eighteenth century—is unpersuasive. Ng’s reasoning rests on two Qing developments: 1) in the 1730s, the government ordered families to confine their mad relatives even when they had committed no crime; 2) in the eighteenth century, legislators meted out increasingly severe sentences upon those who killed someone in a fit of madness. Ng argued that these measures, which she attributed to a more-assumed-than-argued Manchu obsession with social control, represented a sea change in Chinese attitudes toward madness. But the “from illness to deviance” thesis is largely an artifact of the way Ng staged her documents. $ ^{4} $ When Ng claims that legal views replaced medical ones, it is in fact her analytical gaze that leaps from medical to legal documents, never to look back. As I shall show in chapters 11 to 15, medical, legal, religious, and popular views often overlapped, ...

This tension is probably part of what still makes Foucault’s book uniquely attractive. Meaning and referentiality are immensely difficult philosophical issues that I will not attempt to solve. These scripts were templates for describing reality, but also interpretive frameworks that guided people’s interventions in fluid processes. Physicians deployed such scripts when they treated patients, judicial officials when they sentenced crimes. To avoid making generalizations that will predictably not apply to all people of all periods, I use the notion of “prototype.” $ ^{22} $ Most definitions are prototypical in that they mark out the most typical members of a given category (“tables,” “burrowing animals”). Categories contain more than prototypes, but their members gradually radiate away from the prototypical hub. A three-legged chair is thus a less prototypical chair than a four-legged one, the platypus a less prototypical mammal than the elephant. Since prototypes also apply to the way we interpret events, I will call “prototypical scripts” the recurring scenarios by which experts explained how illnesses or crimes took place. The script “internal depletion occurs → Wind intrudes → illness ensues,” for instance, was omnipresent in medical treatises from the seventh and eighth centuries (see 3.3). New scripts emphasizing the pathology of mucus gradually replaced Wind-based ones and came to dominate ...

evidence also exists about how madness was handled in less literate social circles, often but not always through religious ritual. In the interrelated ways in which they explained and handled madness, the three contemporaneous fields of homicide law, medicine, and trials for sedition form a manifold cultural formation, a network of discourses and practices that is most profitably studied as a whole if we want to appreciate what any of them meant. $ ^{23} $ To understand why Qing law discussed insanity and handled “mad criminals” the way it did, we need to compare it to the medical and religious approaches that were also current at the time. And we shall appreciate the nature of medical treatments all the better if we grasp the ways in which other fields handled mad behavior. Since madness was never an important focus of medical reflections, changes in medical views of madness are difficult to study. Kuang was the object of no specialized literature, and no debate concerning it had any major impact on doctrinal developments. But since late imperial physicians were expected to treat all disorders—from furor to furuncles—the way they handled any illness usually depended on how they understood pathogenesis, vitality, and general therapeutic principles. unmatched_anchors.source_text: Was madness a single object, then? And were words like diankuang and jengbing all synonyms? No. Although both legal and medical specialists counted on the easy recognizability of madness, they transposed daily practice and local labels into specialized categories and scripts. $ ^{2} $ Penal denominations and illness names channeled phenomena into understandable and manageable forms. Physicians, for instance, tended to reduce to organic imbalances some problems that had probably emerged through social interactions. They asserted their competence by claiming to understand the organic roots of emotional discomforts and behavioral disorders. This reductionism created—or “enacted”—a different kind of madness than the one found in ritual therapies or judicial practice. $ ^{3} $ As such, physicians faced a different object than legal officials.

Legal officials did not understand madness in the same way as physicians. Although the legal object “madness illness” borrowed from medical and popular conceptions, it evolved to suit the concerns and categories of the legal field. At first, Qing law targeted only one kind of act (homicides), not madness or mad people in general. A killing was considered to have been committed “because of madness” when the killer had had no cognizance—and had no recollection—of his act. $ ^{4} $ This lack of awareness was what convinced early Qing officials that mad killers should not be physically punished. $ ^{5} $

Legal officials did not understand madness in the same way as physicians. Although the legal object “madness illness” borrowed from medical and popular conceptions, it evolved to suit the concerns and categories of the legal field. At first, Qing law targeted only one kind of act (homicides), not madness or mad people in general. A killing was considered to have been committed “because of madness” when the killer had had no cognizance—and had no recollection—of his act. $ ^{4} $ This lack of awareness was what convinced early Qing officials that mad killers should not be physically punished. $ ^{5} $

Fiction could be put to many more uses. One feature of mad people in medical texts and popular perception was the peculiarity of their words. How did fictional sources portray mad speech? My impression is that mad characters in Chinese stories were usually misrecognized as mad before being revealed to be something else—playful immortals in disguise, hermits or visionaries, or people in the grip of real demons. Madness thus served to dissimulate something else, sometimes from the reader, but most often from other characters in the story. $ ^{6} $

page_contexts.ocr_excerpt: ## 16 ## Madness multiple $ ^{1} $ Language, illness, and behavior in law and medicine Madness is not an entity or an essence, but an ascribed characteristic of various acts, words, or people. In practice—while treating an illness, judging a legal case, or taking care of a sick relative at home—people could label specific men or women, acts or words, as being diankuang 癫狂, fengdian 風顛, or fengbing 瘋病. But these labels were contextual. Several different words could thus be used to refer to the same person. Depending on what they wanted to emphasize, villagers could call a man's manic behavior kuang 狂, the oddness of his deportment dian 顛, his unrealistic aspirations chi 癡, or his more uncanny acts guai 怪. Conversely, two different terms did not have to refer to mutually exclusive realities. A woman in rural Henan, for instance, could easily accept that the legal category fengbing (“madness illness”) applied to her son even if she labeled him dian and considered him possessed. Even judicial reports used fengbing side by side with non-technical terms like fengdian 風癲 or medical expressions like “mucus confusing the openings of the Heart” 痰迷心竅 as if they were all equivalent. My focus on practical usage eased the problem of definitions, but even as I tried not to base my investigations on a universal, I had to struggle with how swiftly and easily Qing people—be it a man who sought m ...

of labels to be recognizable from one field to another, but it also suggests that most people agreed about what madness was. Was madness a single object, then? And were words like diankuang and jengbing all synonyms? No. Although both legal and medical specialists counted on the easy recognizability of madness, they transposed daily practice and local labels into specialized categories and scripts. $ ^{2} $ Penal denominations and illness names channeled phenomena into understandable and manageable forms. Physicians, for instance, tended to reduce to organic imbalances some problems that had probably emerged through social interactions. They asserted their competence by claiming to understand the organic roots of emotional discomforts and behavioral disorders. This reductionism created—or “enacted”—a different kind of madness than the one found in ritual therapies or judicial practice. $ ^{3} $ As such, physicians faced a different object than legal officials. I tried to develop an approach that would allow me to understand both the obviousness of village madmen and the complexities of technical objects like legal fenghing and medical kuang. For this purpose, I tried to show how these common words acquired specific referents through medical and legal practices. This meant showing how, despite the common equivalence of terms like kuang and fengdian in daily contexts, some fields ...

rigor in observation or as a response to the supposedly universal problem of what to do with violent mad people. “Killing because of madness” was created in the 1660s when some officials started considering as legally relevant the fact that some killings were committed in a state of madness. Legal officials did not understand madness in the same way as physicians. Although the legal object “madness illness” borrowed from medical and popular conceptions, it evolved to suit the concerns and categories of the legal field. At first, Qing law targeted only one kind of act (homicides), not madness or mad people in general. A killing was considered to have been committed “because of madness” when the killer had had no cognizance—and had no recollection—of his act. $ ^{4} $ This lack of awareness was what convinced early Qing officials that mad killers should not be physically punished. $ ^{5} $ When the Ministry of Punishments ordered all families to confine “people with madness illness” preventively (first in 1689, and then more rigorously in the 1730s and 1760s), it was thinking of madness from the point of view of its occasional outcome—a homicide committed during a fit (see 11.4). My impression is that mad characters in Chinese stories were usually misrecognized as mad before being revealed to be something else—playful immortals in disguise, hermits or visionaries, or people in the grip of real demons. Madness thus served to dissimulate something else, sometimes from the reader, but most often from other characters in the story. $ ^{6} $ Another issue I barely addressed is how educated people dealt with their own mental torments. What did men who resorted to neither medicine nor exorcism do when they felt themselves becoming crazy or saw their peers lose sanity? We could probably find several cases like that of Xu Nianzu 許念祖 (1655-1698), a scholar who committed suicide after repeated bouts of despair that his friends and correspondents referred to as madness (for references, see 8.5, p. 344n50). unmatched_anchors.source_text: There were effectively no “types of madness” in Chinese medicine, just one madness—kuang or diankuang—with many symptoms. Historians usually interpret dian as a form of madness distinct from kuang (see 1: 5n9), but I will argue that a clear symptomatic distinction between dian and kuang only crystallized in the nineteenth century. $ ^{5} $ Kuang referred to various disorders of perception, affect, language, and especially behavior. People were labeled kuang when they lost some or all social competence—a woman ran outside naked, a man became delirious or took himself for a god. Physicians may have focused on patent mad behavior because they were only called to treat men and women whom their relatives saw as patently mad. Most feelings of mental discomfort were probably not reported to doctors, and thus rarely appeared in medical documents. I will call the symptoms of kuang “psycho-behavioral.”

Categories and scripts channeled phenomena into intelligible and manageable forms; they also guided perception and suggested methods to tackle the reality they purported to describe. Broadly speaking, medical specialists attributed mad symptoms either to invasion by external forces (“deviant Qi”, Wind, ghosts) or to internal imbalances strong enough to provoke illness. $ ^{7} $ Doctors claimed they could cure their patients by expelling these external invaders or redressing these pathogenic imbalances. Because of this emphasis on dynamic situations and a general therapeutic optimism, the medical tradition considered permanent disorders anomalous. Chinese physicians therefore rarely medicalized personality and almost never called patients “madmen” (狂人 or 瘋子).

      "ocr_excerpt": "designated madness, but I reject the possibility that feng bing referred to another kind of madness than dian 癲 and kuang 狂. ## The Qing judicial system and the documents it produced Criminal investigations usually began when someone brought an illegal act to the attention of the local magistrate, who, in addition to being the highest-ranked local administrator, was also in charge of judicial matters. When a suspicious death occurred, the magistrate brought a coroner with him to examine the corpse and ascertain the cause of death. If necessary, the magistrate also sent deputies to apprehend the killer. Although the magistrate could start to interrogate witnesses at once, the formal trial only started when the “criminal”—as a person under trial was called—and the witnesses had all been assembled. In Chinese imperial law, no indictment told a person what charges he or she faced. Instead, the magistrate investigated the “circumstances” (qing 情) of a criminal act and then decided under what law that crime should be sentenced. He then composed a report (xiang 詳) recounting the entire affair and specifying the sentence (ni 擬) that went with the law that covered this particular act. $ ^{4 ..."
    },
    {
      "page_no": 434,
      "ocr_excerpt": "The key to the widening use of feng 瘋 to mean “madness” was its phonetic equivalence with feng 風 (“Wind”) and the age-old association of the sound feng with madness in expressions like fengkuang (風狂, “Wind madness”), fengdian (風癲, “Wind dian”), and xin feng 心風 (“Heart Wind”). By the twelfth century the sound feng (“Wind”) had been associated with madness for so long that it had come to mean madness even when it was not followed by either kuang or dian nor disambiguated by the addition of the “illness radical” (疒) to yield the form 瘋. $ ^{22} $ One “crazy monk brother” (風僧哥) was mentioned in a twelfth-century collection of anecdotes. $ ^{23} $ The term fa feng 發風 (“fit of Wind” = “fit of madness”) also appeared in a Yuan play titled A Pair of Battling Quacks (Shuang dou yi 雙鬥醫). $ ^{24} $ As mainstream medicine stopped attributing madness to Wind, $ ^{25} $ feng in the above compounds became an etymological reminder rather than an etiological marker. In other words, the term fengkuang no longer implied a belief that madness was caused by Wind, just as “lunatic” no longer refers to people who have lost their mind because of the moon. $ ^{26} $ In the late seventeenth century, for exa ..."
    },
    {

      "ocr_excerpt": "Feng bing (written 瘋病) may first have sounded as inelgant in Chinese as it does in its literal English translation \"madness illness.\" The continuous use of fengdian 風顛 and fengkuang 風狂 (both written with the character for \"Wind\" 風) in legal documents well into the eighteenth century suggests that the form 瘋 still rarely referred to madness in the seventeenth and eighteenth centuries. $ ^{31} $ In 1736, one memorialist cited the law on \"killing because of madness\" as fengdian sharen 風顛殺人 (instead of fengbing sharen 瘋病殺人), and as late as 1816, a copyist of the Ministry of Punishments was reprimanded for writing yin feng shan as 因風殺人 (literally, \"killing because of Wind\") in the summary of a memorial. $ ^{32} $ A few scholars have suggested that the choice of fengbing over common medical terms like fengdian, kuang, or diankuang shows that Qing law targeted another kind of madness than medicine did.   "chapter_body_excerpt": "# 10 ## Prologue to Part Two Madness and mad people in the Qing dynasty have been studied mostly in a legal context. $ ^{1} $ The easy availability and relative intelligibility of legal documents vis-à-vis medical ones have undoubtedly contributed to this trend. $ ^{2} $ Following pioneers like Chen Hsiu-fen and Angelika Messner, I showed in Part One that Qing medical approaches to madness were also a legitimate topic. In Part Two, I want to show that the study of Qing legislative and judicial activities surrounding madness can be improved, and gains from being compared with the many other ways in which mad acts, words, and people were handled in both technical and non-technical contexts. In this prologue and the next few chapters, I will often propose brief comparisons with the medical field, leaving more systematic reflections for Chapter 16, the general conclusion. But before I turn to comparisons, let me first explain how the Qing legal field functioned and what kinds of documents it produced. Doing so will help to understand how the object \"madness illness\" (feng bing 瘋病) emerged from concrete legal practices. I then present the ways in which dynasties prior to the Qing dealt with homicides committed in a fit of insanity. $ ^{3} $ Then I probe the origin of the term \"madness illness\" by which Qing law designated madness, but I reject the possibility that feng bing referred to another kind of madness than dian 癲 and kuang 狂. ## The Qing judicial system and the documents it produced Criminal investigations usually began when someone brought an illegal act to the attention of the local magistrate, who, in addition to being the highest-ranked local administrator, was also in charge of judicial matters. When a suspicious death occurred, the magistrate brought a coroner ..."

In Chinese imperial law, no indictment told a person what charges he or she faced. Instead, the magistrate investigated the “circumstances” (qing 情) of a criminal act and then decided under what law that crime should be sentenced. He then composed a report (xiang 詳) recounting the entire affair and specifying the sentence (ni 擬) that went with the law that covered this particular act. $ ^{4} $ He sent this report to his immediate superior the prefect.

page_contexts.ocr_excerpt: designated madness, but I reject the possibility that feng bing referred to another kind of madness than dian 癲 and kuang 狂. ## The Qing judicial system and the documents it produced Criminal investigations usually began when someone brought an illegal act to the attention of the local magistrate, who, in addition to being the highest-ranked local administrator, was also in charge of judicial matters. When a suspicious death occurred, the magistrate brought a coroner with him to examine the corpse and ascertain the cause of death. If necessary, the magistrate also sent deputies to apprehend the killer. Although the magistrate could start to interrogate witnesses at once, the formal trial only started when the “criminal”—as a person under trial was called—and the witnesses had all been assembled. In Chinese imperial law, no indictment told a person what charges he or she faced. Instead, the magistrate investigated the “circumstances” (qing 情) of a criminal act and then decided under what law that crime should be sentenced. He then composed a report (xiang 詳) recounting the entire affair and specifying the sentence (ni 擬) that went with the law that covered this particular act. $ ^{4} $ He sent this report to his immediate superior the prefect. In a “re-trial” (覆審), the prefect reviewed the entire case and could go as far as to re-interrogate the accused and all the witnesses. $ ...

The key to the widening use of feng 瘋 to mean “madness” was its phonetic equivalence with feng 風 (“Wind”) and the age-old association of the sound feng with madness in expressions like fengkuang (風狂, “Wind madness”), fengdian (風癲, “Wind dian”), and xin feng 心風 (“Heart Wind”). By the twelfth century the sound feng (“Wind”) had been associated with madness for so long that it had come to mean madness even when it was not followed by either kuang or dian nor disambiguated by the addition of the “illness radical” (疒) to yield the form 瘋. $ ^{22} $ One “crazy monk brother” (風僧哥) was mentioned in a twelfth-century collection of anecdotes. $ ^{23} $ The term fa feng 發風 (“fit of Wind” = “fit of madness”) also appeared in a Yuan play titled A Pair of Battling Quacks (Shuang dou yi 雙鬥醫). $ ^{24} $ As mainstream medicine stopped attributing madness to Wind, $ ^{25} $ feng in the above compounds became an etymological reminder rather than an etiological marker. In other words, the term fengkuang no longer implied a belief that madness was caused by Wind, just as “lunatic” no longer refers to people who have lost their mind because of the moon. $ ^{26} $ In the late seventeenth century, for example, a physician from Huizhou in Anhui explained in his medical treatise that kuang people were often called fengzi 風子, a term that became widely used in Qing times, though it tended to be written 瘋子 ...

Feng bing (written 瘋病) may first have sounded as inelgant in Chinese as it does in its literal English translation "madness illness." The continuous use of fengdian 風顛 and fengkuang 風狂 (both written with the character for "Wind" 風) in legal documents well into the eighteenth century suggests that the form 瘋 still rarely referred to madness in the seventeenth and eighteenth centuries. $ ^{31} $ In 1736, one memorialist cited the law on "killing because of madness" as fengdian sharen 風顛殺人 (instead of fengbing sharen 瘋病殺人), and as late as 1816, a copyist of the Ministry of Punishments was reprimanded for writing yin feng shan as 因風殺人 (literally, "killing because of Wind") in the summary of a memorial. $ ^{32} $ A few scholars have suggested that the choice of fengbing over common medical terms like fengdian, kuang, or diankuang shows that Qing law targeted another kind of madness than medicine did. Angelika Messner (2000: 22), for example, has suggested that Qing laws did not target "insanity as illness," but a kind of non-medical "raging madness" (rasenden Wabnsinn). $ ^{33} $ This distinction does not hold.
